entries.name: Maturing
entries: Wyrmlings wander the foothills where they hatch, challenging any creatures they meet. They challenge others in feats of strength, balance, or speed, or in riddles, witty remarks, or even games of strategy, like chess. By the time the dragon is young, it bears a few scars from its first challenges and becomes choosier in which challenges it accepts. It starts looking for its lair, usually settling on one by the time it is an adult. As an adult, the copper dragon’s renown means others can seek out the dragon for challenges. By the time copper dragons are ancients, their hides are scarred and marked by centuries of battle. Ancient copper dragons will sometimes challenge each other for prized armor and weapons in their hoards, and many powerful artifacts change claws in such duels.

entries.name: A Copper Dragon’s Lair
entries: Copper dragons make their lairs in fortresses, castles, and other defensible structures built by humanoids, going so far as to hire humanoids to create one if a suitable fortification isn’t available. At the heart of these heavily defended forts is a great hall where the copper dragon sleeps surrounded

entries.name: Gold Dragons
entries: Dragons of Hope and Majesty

items.name: Allies:
items.entry: Typically encountered solo, with others of its kind, or rarely works with other creatures.

entries: Gold dragons work to make the world a better place. The most powerful of the metallic dragons, these awe-inspiring dragons strive to protect that which is good and bend fate toward a brighter future. Their kind dispositions don't prevent gold dragons from engaging in combat when necessary, though, and they exhale brilliant flames and weakening magic to rout their foes.

Gold dragons favor grasslands and pristine forests, frequently dwelling near awe-inspiring natural wonders or guarding monuments from ancient civilizations. In their lairs, gold dragons hoard coins and gems, but they frequently put their treasure to use in pursuit of greater goals. They often use their riches to buy rare lore books, pay informants, or patronize idealistic adventurers.

entries.name: Gold Dragon Lairs
entries: Gold dragons make their homes in places of natural and magical wonder.

Nodding its majestic head, the sinuous golden dragon exudes a nearly overwhelming aura of peace and tranquility. Two long horns curve back from its head, and long whisker-like barbels hang from its serenely smiling face.

entries.name: Enlightenment Seekers
entries: According to gold dragon myths, the first of their kind were cleaved from the side of their draconic deity by the claw of a great evil. Since that time, these dragons have searched for a way to return to godhood by achieving enlightenment through practiced mastery of martial arts, magic, and meditation. Gold dragons rarely become involved in the world, preferring to act as teachers and guides from their ornate temples.

entries.name: Hoard of Scriptures
entries: To gold dragons, scrolls, books, and manuals regarding the improvement of body and spirit are the only items worth collecting—though some do admit to a mild fondness for gold as an object of meditation. They dispense the wisdom of their hoards very selectively, as they believe that too much knowledge learned too fast is dangerous.

entries.name: Old Sweepers, Serene Madams
entries: Gold dragons often disguise themselves as humble, elderly humanoids. In some temples, the temple leader is a mere mortal, and the real dragon is but the elderly sweeper, dispensing critical wisdom to those wise enough to listen. In others, the master of the temple library is the gold dragon, who speaks only rarely, and then through a human monk or priest.

entries.name: A Gold Dragon’s Lair
entries: Gold dragons make their homes in spiritual temples and sometimes libraries they create or that are created by humanoids. These temples are often in hard‑to‑reach places that require dedication and self-discipline to reach. Monks and priests flock to these temples, seeking enlightenment, and take care of the day‑to-day upkeep and defense of the temples, the training of students, and other necessities. On initiative count 20 (losing initiative ties), the dragon can take a lair action to cause one of the following effects; the dragon can’t use the same effect two rounds in a row:

entries.name: • Blinding Light
entries: A brilliant flash of scorching light surrounds up to three creatures the dragon can see within 120 feet of it. Each target must succeed on a DC 15 DEX save or take 13 (3d8) fire damage and be blinded until the end of its next turn.

entries.name: • Boundless Wisdom
entries: The wisdom of generations briefly fills the mind of one creature the dragon can see within 60 feet of it. If the target is friendly, it has advantage on ability checks, attack rolls, and saves until initiative count 20 of the next round, as its body moves and reacts from generations of honed training. If the target is hostile, it must succeed on a DC 15 CHA save or be incapacitated until the end of its next turn, as its mind is overwhelmed by the flood of information.

entries.name: Gold Dragons
entries: Dragons of Hope and Majesty

items.name: Allies:
items.entry: Other gold dragons, Humanoids dedicated to enlightenment and the pursuit of knowledge

entries: Gold dragons work to make the world a better place. The most powerful of the metallic dragons, these awe-inspiring dragons strive to protect that which is good and bend fate toward a brighter future. Their kind dispositions don't prevent gold dragons from engaging in combat when necessary, though, and they exhale brilliant flames and weakening magic to rout their foes.

Gold dragons favor grasslands and pristine forests, frequently dwelling near awe-inspiring natural wonders or guarding monuments from ancient civilizations. In their lairs, gold dragons hoard coins and gems, but they frequently put their treasure to use in pursuit of greater goals. They often use their riches to buy rare lore books, pay informants, or patronize idealistic adventurers.

entries.name: Gold Dragon Lairs
entries: Gold dragons make their homes in places of natural and magical wonder.

Nodding its majestic head, the sinuous golden dragon exudes a nearly overwhelming aura of peace and tranquility. Two long horns curve back from its head, and long whisker-like barbels hang from its serenely smiling face.

entries.name: Enlightenment Seekers
entries: According to gold dragon myths, the first of their kind were cleaved from the side of their draconic deity by the claw of a great evil. Since that time, these dragons have searched for a way to return to godhood by achieving enlightenment through practiced mastery of martial arts, magic, and meditation. Gold dragons rarely become involved in the world, preferring to act as teachers and guides from their ornate temples.

entries.name: Hoard of Scriptures
entries: To gold dragons, scrolls, books, and manuals regarding the improvement of body and spirit are the only items worth collecting—though some do admit to a mild fondness for gold as an object of meditation. They dispense the wisdom of their hoards very selectively, as they believe that too much knowledge learned too fast is dangerous.

entries.name: Old Sweepers, Serene Madams
entries: Gold dragons often disguise themselves as humble, elderly humanoids. In some temples, the temple leader is a mere mortal, and the real dragon is but the elderly sweeper, dispensing critical wisdom to those wise enough to listen. In others, the master of the temple library is the gold dragon, who speaks only rarely, and then through a human monk or priest.

entries.name: A Gold Dragon’s Lair
entries: Gold dragons make their homes in spiritual temples and sometimes libraries they create or that are created by humanoids. These temples are often in hard‑to‑reach places that require dedication and self-discipline to reach. Monks and priests flock to these temples, seeking enlightenment, and take care of the day‑to-day upkeep and defense of the temples, the training of students, and other necessities. On initiative count 20 (losing initiative ties), the dragon can take a lair action to cause one of the following effects; the dragon can’t use the same effect two rounds in a row:

In others, the master of the temple library is the gold dragon, who speaks only rarely, and then through a human monk or priest.

entries.name: Maturing
entries: Gold dragons spend the first few years of their lives observing the ebb and flow of the natural world, eventually entering the world in pursuit of enlightenment. Adult gold dragons settle into or begin creating a temple or great library where they collect, write, and build their hoard of manuscripts, manuals, books, and scrolls. Before long, they begin inviting other enlightenment seekers to their collections. By the time the dragon is ancient, its temple runs smoothly without day-to-day interference from the dragon, and the dragon spends almost all its time in the guise of a temple elder, ready to dispense wisdom to those seeking it. Some ancient dragons spend so much time in non-dragon form that even the keepers of the temples don’t know a dragon lives among them.

entries.name: Green Dragons
entries: Dragons of Deceit and Derision

items.name: Allies:
items.entry: Typically encountered solo, with others of its kind, or rarely works with other creatures.

entries: From forbidden forest depths, green dragons whisper evils into the world and manipulate the lives of those who listen. Elusive, conniving, and egotistical, these chromatic dragons patiently prey on the fears of shorter-lived beings, corrupting and isolating them. Green dragons might lurk amid labyrinthine wildernesses for centuries without revealing themselves; even their most devoted followers might know them only as the voice of the woodlands or a whisper in their dreams.

Despite their might, most green dragons disdain physical violence, viewing combat as servants' work and preferring to trick foes into dangerous or exploitative scenarios. These dragons collect "baubles" that embody their webs of manipulation and serve as tools of extortion, such as compromising documents, family heirlooms, and sentimental treasures.

entries.name: Green Dragon Lairs
entries: Green dragons lair in ancient forests, often shaping stands of massive trees into compounds of interwoven branches, hollow trunks, and caverns amid mighty roots. They might also dwell amid forested ruins, particularly the former homes of those they've conquered.
Green and orange feathers trail down the spine of this green-scaled dragon, and tendrils of acrid vapor curl up from its crocodilian head. It searches the forest underbrush.

entries.name: Forest Marauders
entries: Green dragons prefer to live in ancient woodlands, the better to hide their lairs and to store their enormous wealth. In addition, they are well aware of the scents and seasons of their home terrain and can show a surprising degree of stealth when wandering the woodlands or snaking through the high branches of ancient trees.

entries.name: Emotionless Scientists
entries: Most green dragons are distant and detached scientists. Those who interact with these dragons quickly learn not to attribute feelings or sentiment to the wyrms. The only time they show excessive emotions is when their experiments are threatened, reacting with cold, logical fury.

entries.name: Unfettered Experiments
entries: Insatiably curious about the minute workings of the world, green dragons obsessively study life, growth, and decay. Over time, their experiments begin to demand living subjects, pollute waterways, and create roiling clouds of poison gas. They care little for the impact of their experiments, raiding nearby villages for test subjects and releasing them into the wild once the project is complete.

entries.name: Knowledge Shared
entries: To green dragons, knowledge gained and not shared—or gloated about—might as well not have been gained at all. They frequently gather with others of their kind to share successes, downplay failures, and subtly coax knowledge related to their studies from their draconic peers. Green dragons rarely share their knowledge with non-dragons, believing only draconic minds can truly understand the dragon’s brilliance.

entries.name: Alchemical Hoards
entries: A green dragon’s hoard often contains magical potions, powerful unguents, rare components, tomes of lore, and strange alchemical or magical objects, such as philosopher’s stones or crystal balls. Green dragons keep their hoards well organized, with each item labeled and catalogued. Adventurers can approach a green dragon to exchange exotic components for rare magical potions, though care is needed in the negotiation.

entries.name: A Green Dragon’s Lair
entries: Green dragons establish their lairs in remote caverns, ruins, or buildings in or beneath distant forests. Such lairs might have a hidden entrance below the surface of a nearby lake or river, allowing the dragon a quick—and fireproof—escape from alchemy gone wrong or pesky adventurers. They manipulate the natural features around their lairs to grow towering walls of brambles, impossibly smooth cliffs, or ravines filled with strange waste waters. The lair itself might contain vats and mixing chambers, storage rooms, and fortified prison cells for less-willing participants.

entries.name: Red Dragons
entries: Dragons of Greed and Devastation

items.name: Allies:
items.entry: Duergar, kobolds, and other mountain-dwelling Humanoids willing to serve

entries: Red dragons take whatever they desire and burn to ash anything that stands in their way. These chromatic dragons endlessly desire more—more magic, territory, treasure, or whatever else inflames their cruel ambitions.

Red dragons make their lairs amid perilous cliffs and volcanoes. Within, they amass and fiercely protect hoards of treasure, and many have perfect recall of the hoards' contents and the locations of all they've collected. Should anything go missing, red dragons fly into rages. They don't rest until their treasures are returned and the thieves have burned.

Red dragons believe themselves to be the greatest of all dragons and, by extension, the greatest of all creatures. To them, pillaging and conquering are their right—treasures can find no more honored place than in their hoards, and other creatures are privileged to serve them.

entries.name: Red Dragon Lairs
entries: Red dragons make their lairs in smoldering, unapproachable places such as volcanic mountains, burning wastelands, and ruins they've stolen from other creatures.
A mighty dragon with bright red scales swoops down from the mountain, setting the countryside ablaze with its fiery breath.

entries.name: Avaricious and Territorial
entries: All dragons hoard treasure, but the greed of the red dragon reigns supreme. They prefer quantity over quality, prominently displaying stolen gold and the skulls of their foes. In twisting tunnels in the fiery belly of the earth, they protect their fabulous wealth with cunning traps and terrible guardians. Red dragons brook no incursions into their territory, which can stretch for miles around their lairs.

entries.name: Dominating Pride
entries: Red dragons truly believe they are the mightiest of all dragons and superior to all other creatures. They are quick to boast of their own greatness and happy to demonstrate their supremacy by fang or fire. This pride makes them tyrannical, and many red dragons delight in forcing tribute or service from lesser creatures.

entries.name: Incendiary Rage
entries: The magical core of fire at the heart of every red dragon both generates their incinerating breath and makes them quick to anger. They take offense at even slight provocation, flying into devastating rages that last until their fury is vented.

entries.name: A Red Dragon’s Lair
entries: A red dragon favors high mountains, claiming snowy peaks, fuming volcanoes, or sprawling ancient ruins. A red dragon’s immense strength, agile flight, and comfort in blistering temperatures make its lair difficult to traverse for most other creatures. They are quick to boast of their own greatness and happy to demonstrate their supremacy by fang or fire. This pride makes them tyrannical, and many red dragons delight in forcing tribute or service from lesser creatures.

entries.name: Incendiary Rage
entries: The magical core of fire at the heart of every red dragon both generates their incinerating breath and makes them quick to anger. They take offense at even slight provocation, flying into devastating rages that last until their fury is vented.

entries.name: A Red Dragon’s Lair
entries: A red dragon favors high mountains, claiming snowy peaks, fuming volcanoes, or sprawling ancient ruins. A red dragon’s immense strength, agile flight, and comfort in blistering temperatures make its lair difficult to traverse for most other creatures. From its skyward vantage point, a red dragon peers down at

entries.name: Red Dragons
entries: Dragons of Greed and Devastation

items.name: Allies:
items.entry: Fire giants and duergar, kobolds, and other mountain-dwelling Humanoids willing to serve

entries: Red dragons take whatever they desire and burn to ash anything that stands in their way. These chromatic dragons endlessly desire more—more magic, territory, treasure, or whatever else inflames their cruel ambitions.

Red dragons make their lairs amid perilous cliffs and volcanoes. Within, they amass and fiercely protect hoards of treasure, and many have perfect recall of the hoards' contents and the locations of all they've collected. Should anything go missing, red dragons fly into rages. They don't rest until their treasures are returned and the thieves have burned.

Red dragons believe themselves to be the greatest of all dragons and, by extension, the greatest of all creatures. To them, pillaging and conquering are their right—treasures can find no more honored place than in their hoards, and other creatures are privileged to serve them.

entries.name: Red Dragon Lairs
entries: Red dragons make their lairs in smoldering, unapproachable places such as volcanic mountains, burning wastelands, and ruins they've stolen from other creatures.
A mighty dragon with bright red scales swoops down from the mountain, setting the countryside ablaze with its fiery breath.

entries.name: Avaricious and Territorial
entries: All dragons hoard treasure, but the greed of the red dragon reigns supreme. They prefer quantity over quality, prominently displaying stolen gold and the skulls of their foes. In twisting tunnels in the fiery belly of the earth, they protect their fabulous wealth with cunning traps and terrible guardians. Red dragons brook no incursions into their territory, which can stretch for miles around their lairs.

entries.name: Dominating Pride
entries: Red dragons truly believe they are the mightiest of all dragons and superior to all other creatures. They are quick to boast of their own greatness and happy to demonstrate their supremacy by fang or fire. This pride makes them tyrannical, and many red dragons delight in forcing tribute or service from lesser creatures.

This pride makes them tyrannical, and many red dragons delight in forcing tribute or service from lesser creatures.

entries.name: Incendiary Rage
entries: The magical core of fire at the heart of every red dragon both generates their incinerating breath and makes them quick to anger. They take offense at even slight provocation, flying into devastating rages that last until their fury is vented.

entries.name: Maturing
entries: Red wyrmlings hatch on remote mountain cliffs and peaks far from the lairs of other dragons. Even at that age, they are quick to carve out a territory, attempting to subjugate any who dare enter their territory. Many wyrmlings don’t survive past this age, their aggression often exceeding their ability to win the fights they start. By the time the dragon is young, it bears several scars, each a story of hard-won victory and every inch of territory maintained. During this time, the dragon starts to hoard treasures it gathers from frightened or slain travelers, preferring gold and all things that glitter. As an adult, the red dragon has claimed a lair and already accumulated significant wealth, aggressively protecting it and the surrounding territory. At this age, some red dragons make pacts with nearby humanoids, offering—and usually providing—protection in exchange for regular tributes of gold or meat. Ancient red dragons typically have several groups or settlements of humanoids in their service and spend their days gloating over past glories atop a mountain of gold.

entries.name: A Red Dragon’s Lair
entries: A red dragon favors high mountains, claiming snowy peaks, fuming volcanoes, or sprawling ancient ruins. A red dragon’s immense strength, agile flight, and comfort in blistering temperatures make its lair difficult to traverse for most other creatures. From its skyward vantage point, a red dragon peers down at

entries.name: Silver Dragons
entries: Dragons of Courage and Fairness
Silver dragons work to preserve peace and encourage greatness. They try to live as examples of decency while remaining watchful against evil.

Silver dragons typically dwell amid snow-capped mountains, though aspirations and congeniality drive some to instead live among cosmopolitan societies. Disguised as humanoids, they ally with artists, historians, knights, and humble leaders who learn from the past to create better futures.
Silver dragons take inspiration from legendary heroes and have grand ambitions. Many collect treasures that reflect these interests, such as histories, ancient art, and the gear of famous champions.

entries.name: Silver Dragon Lairs
entries: Silver dragons typically lair in picturesque mountain retreats or on sculpted cloud "islands."

This dragon shimmers like polished silver as it moves. Large scales like layered breastplates run the length of its spine, and a pair of horns curves forward from its skull. A frosty chill hangs in the air around it.

entries.name: For the Greater Good
entries: Silver dragons believe in promoting the greatest good for the greatest number of beings, especially dragons. This stance can sometimes lead the dragon to commit or condone acts many would consider less than good. However, the dragon’s actions operate across centuries and millennia, and it knows the steps it takes in the short term will benefit many more in the long term. Silver dragons focus on the larger picture and life as a whole, combating great evils and calamities on cosmic time scales.

entries.name: Historical Hoard
entries: Silver dragon hoards are museums of their favored cultures. Their lairs are filled with artifacts from these civilizations—coins, fine arts, tapestries, manuscripts, epic poems, and historical treatises. The hoard might also contain the intact sarcophagus of the first ruler of the first nation the silver dragon watched over, the armor of an adopted civilization’s first great warrior, or the favored trinket of the first creature the dragon befriended.

entries.name: A Silver Dragon’s Lair
entries: Silver dragon lairs are always found at high altitudes. Caverns amid snowy peaks are common. However, the most prized locales for a silver dragon’s lair are remnants

entries.name: Silver Dragons
entries: Dragons of Courage and Fairness

items.name: Allies:
items.entry: Other silver dragons and Humanoids, especially from young civilizations

entries.name: Kindhearted Watchers
entries: Looking down from their mountain perches, silver dragons see it as their duty to befriend and protect other sentient beings in their territory. Aloof and watchful, they watch over ages as civilizations develop, occasionally adopting disguises to provide guiding nudges. Elders warn young silver dragons to avoid direct entanglement with humanoid affairs. Some can’t help themselves, and inadvertently become weapons of war, or give rise to magical, royal bloodlines.

entries.name: Maturing
entries: Silver dragons spend the first few years of their lives with their parents, learning about various creatures and the draconic role in maintaining the betterment of life across millennia. By the time the dragon is young, it has developed a fondness for a particular creature, people, or culture and seeks to study its new obsession from a distance, often in the guise of a beast of burden or traveler. By the time the dragon is an adult, it has carved out its own small lair where it maintains a few souvenirs from beloved places or people and records their achievements or failings. With vast patience, ancient silver dragons often spend decades resting in their lairs, coming out every few years to check on the progress of nearby lands and encouraging small changes to foster a better world.

entries.name: A Silver Dragon’s Lair
entries: Silver dragon lairs are always found at high altitudes. Caverns amid snowy peaks are common. However, the most prized locales for a silver dragon’s lair are remnants

entries.name: Void Dragon Wyrmling
entries: Young Dragon of Starlit Nothingness

items.name: Allies:
items.entry: Selang, voidlings, and other void-related creatures

entries: A dragon seemingly formed of the night sky has bright white stars for eyes. Lesser stars twinkle in the firmament of the dragon’s body.

entries.name: Children of the Stars
entries: Void dragons drift through the empty spaces beyond the boundaries of the mortal world and wander between the stars. They are aloof, mingling only with the otherworldly beings that live above and beyond the world, including the incarnate forms of the stars themselves.

entries.name: Witnesses to the Void
entries: Void dragons are intensely knowledgeable creatures, but they have seen too much, lingering at the edge of the Void itself. They carry a piece of that nothing with them, and it flows out of them to break the minds of lesser beings when the dragons fly into a rage.

entries.name: Voracious Scholars
entries: Despite their removed existence and strange quirks, void dragons still hoard treasure. Gems that glitter like the stars of their home are particularly prized. Their crowning piece, however, is knowledge. Void dragons jealously hoard scraps of forbidden and forgotten lore of any kind and spend most of their time at home poring over these treasures.

entries.name: A Void Dragon’s Lair
entries: Void dragons make their lairs deep in the freezing, airless space between the stars. When a void dragon claims a home elsewhere, it prefers towering mountain peaks, valleys, or ruins at high elevation with a clear view of the sky. When encountered in its lair, an adult void dragon has a challenge rating of 17, and an ancient void dragon has a challenge rating of 23.

entries.name: Young Void Dragon
entries: Dragon of Starlit Nothingness

entries.name: White Dragons
entries: Dragons of Cold and Cruelty

Among the most primal chromatic dragons, white dragons prioritize survival over all. Life is harsh and uncertain in the arctic expanses, glacial heights, and frozen seas where these dragons dwell. White dragons fiercely protect their territories, scouring the frigid regions for food and evidence of trespassers. Most white dragons ignore the plots of smaller creatures and other dragons, concerning themselves only with their own survival.

White dragons create lairs to defend themselves from other deadly arctic creatures and from dangerous natural conditions. Within these shelters, white dragons hoard testaments to their superiority, such as monstrous skulls, the gear of defeated rivals, and curiosities that capture their interest. To protect such treasure, white dragons coax ice to form over their hoards or sink their wealth in frigid pools. For white dragons, each piece of treasure embodies a victory—the details of which inflate as these dragons age.

entries.name: White Dragon Lairs
entries: White dragons brood in bitterly cold lairs clawed from stone and ice.
The wings and pearlescent scales of this dragon are tinged with blue, and its eyes sparkle an icy blue as it opens its jaw and exhales a blast of wintry air.

entries.name: Primeval Hunters
entries: Animalistic and instinct-driven, white dragons are as much a force of nature as the blizzards of their arctic homes. They hunt for sport, killing more than they can eat and storing frozen kills as snacks for later. When faced with greater challenges, white dragons fight dirty, using slippery ice, sheer glacial cliffs, and furious snowstorms or spring thunderstorms to their advantage against intruders.

entries.name: Vainglorious
entries: White dragons think highly of themselves and readily boast about their achievements to other dragons, especially other white dragons. Because of this, white dragons often seek out powerful opponents, as the more powerful a slain opponent, the more a dragon can boast to its brethren. Clever adventurers that speak with a white dragon can play to the dragon’s vanity, praising its skill and prowess or challenging it to perform seemingly impossible tasks.

entries.name: Trophy Hoards
entries: Trophies are important to white dragons and are integral parts of their hoards. The bodies of frozen foes, rent and battered armor, bent weapons, and similar items showcasing the dragon’s accomplishments fill its lair. White dragons have little use or concern for sparkling treasure, but they recognize when a creature goes to great lengths to protect something. They proudly display items taken from creatures that gave their lives to protect the items, not always understanding the significance of the item but taking pride in slaying its protector. This leads white dragon lairs to be filled with an eclectic collection of weapons, religious relics, armor, and magic items.

entries.name: White Dragons
entries: Dragons of Cold and Cruelty

items.name: Allies:
items.entry: Typically encountered solo, with others of its kind, or rarely works with other creatures.

entries.name: Maturing
entries: White dragons care for their young for the first year of a wyrmling’s life, teaching the wyrmling how to hunt, store frozen prey, and create trophies. Eventually, however, the wyrmling’s growing sense of self and self-importance clashes with the egos of its parents, and all part ways. By the time the dragon is young, it has carved out an icy cave for itself and claimed a small swath of arctic or high mountain terrain as its territory. Adult white dragons spend their days expanding and affirming claim to their territory, often in the form of leaving slaughtered enemies scattered across the landscape. At this age, they also join in gatherings of white dragons, where they boast and display their battle prowess and where adults make alliances for raising strong young. White dragons live violent lives and don’t always survive to become ancients. Paragons of battle, violence, and pride, ancient white dragons are twice as deadly as any blizzard and half as merciful.

By the time the dragon is young, it has carved out an icy cave for itself and claimed a small swath of arctic or high mountain terrain as its territory. Adult white dragons spend their days expanding and affirming claim to their territory, often in the form of leaving slaughtered enemies scattered across the landscape. At this age, they also join in gatherings of white dragons, where they boast and display their battle prowess and where adults make alliances for raising strong young. White dragons live violent lives and don’t always survive to become ancients. Paragons of battle, violence, and pride, ancient white dragons are twice as deadly as any blizzard and half as merciful.

entries.name: White Dragons
entries: Dragons of Cold and Cruelty

items.name: Allies:
items.entry: Typically encountered solo, with others of its kind, or rarely works with other creatures.

entries: Among the most primal chromatic dragons, white dragons prioritize survival over all. Life is harsh and uncertain in the arctic expanses, glacial heights, and frozen seas where these dragons dwell. White dragons fiercely protect their territories, scouring the frigid regions for food and evidence of trespassers. Most white dragons ignore the plots of smaller creatures and other dragons, concerning themselves only with their own survival.

White dragons create lairs to defend themselves from other deadly arctic creatures and from dangerous natural conditions. Within these shelters, white dragons hoard testaments to their superiority, such as monstrous skulls, the gear of defeated rivals, and curiosities that capture their interest. To protect such treasure, white dragons coax ice to form over their hoards or sink their wealth in frigid pools. For white dragons, each piece of treasure embodies a victory—the details of which inflate as these dragons age.

entries.name: White Dragon Lairs
entries: White dragons brood in bitterly cold lairs clawed from stone and ice.
The wings and pearlescent scales of this dragon are tinged with blue, and its eyes sparkle an icy blue as it opens its jaw and exhales a blast of wintry air.

entries.name: Primeval Hunters
entries: Animalistic and instinct-driven, white dragons are as much a force of nature as the blizzards of their arctic homes. They hunt for sport, killing more than they can eat and storing frozen kills as snacks for later. When faced with greater challenges, white dragons fight dirty, using slippery ice, sheer glacial cliffs, and furious snowstorms or spring thunderstorms to their advantage against intruders.

entries.name: Vainglorious
entries: White dragons think highly of themselves and readily boast about their achievements to other dragons, especially other white dragons. Because of this, white dragons often seek out powerful opponents, as the more powerful a slain opponent, the more a dragon can boast to its brethren. Clever adventurers that speak with a white dragon can play to the dragon’s vanity, praising its skill and prowess or challenging it to perform seemingly impossible tasks.

entries.name: Trophy Hoards
entries: Trophies are important to white dragons and are integral parts of their hoards. The bodies of frozen foes, rent and battered armor, bent weapons, and similar items showcasing the dragon’s accomplishments fill its lair. White dragons have little use or concern for sparkling treasure, but they recognize when a creature goes to great lengths to protect something. They proudly display items taken from creatures that gave their lives to protect the items, not always understanding the significance of the item but taking pride in slaying its protector. This leads white dragon lairs to be filled with an eclectic collection of weapons, religious relics, armor, and magic items.

entries.name: Maturing
entries: White dragons care for their young for the first year of a wyrmling’s life, teaching the wyrmling how to hunt, store frozen prey, and create trophies. Eventually, however, the wyrmling’s growing sense of self and self-importance clashes with the egos of its parents, and all part ways. By the time the dragon is young, it has carved out an icy cave for itself and claimed a small swath of arctic or high mountain terrain as its territory. Adult white dragons spend their days expanding and affirming claim to their territory, often in the form of leaving slaughtered enemies scattered across the landscape. At this age, they also join in gatherings of white dragons, where they boast and display their battle prowess and where adults make alliances for raising strong young. White dragons live violent lives and don’t always survive to become ancients. Paragons of battle, violence, and pride, ancient white dragons are twice as deadly as any blizzard and half as merciful.

entries.name: Yellow Dragon Wyrmling
entries: Young Dragon of Sunlit Hunt and Ambush
A shadow ripples the tall grass as a dragon leaps from concealment, its yellow, catlike body blending into the dry grass. It opens its fanged mouth, which glows like the sun.

entries.name: Grassland Dragons
entries: Yellow dragons are solitary hunters in grasslands, hills, and canyons. Their bodies are low-slung, lithe, and catlike—perfect for stalking prey under the cover of tall grass or scrubland foliage. When young, their scales are a buttery yellow, but as they age, they turn a deeper yellow with flecks of tan. The oldest have scales the yellow-gold hue of the late-afternoon sun. This likeness to the sun isn’t superficial—a yellow dragon’s breath weapon is a searing beam of sunlight.

entries.name: Consummate Hunters
entries: Yellow dragons aren’t as animalistic and instinct-driven as their white dragon cousins, but they enjoy days filled with hunting and lounging in the sun, caring little for many of the worldly concerns that plague other dragons. They hunt for sport and like to make it a game, playing with their prey to make the hunt more enjoyable. They crave the chase most of all, and a yellow dragon that isn’t particularly hungry often releases caught prey and resumes the chase. Though winged, yellow dragons find the greatest pleasure in hunting on foot, catching prey unawares in the tall grasses.

entries.name: Ever-Changing Hoards
entries: Yellow dragons are easily distracted, mercurial creatures, and the trinket that held their attention last week might be this week’s junk. They find more delight in novelty rather than preening over the same treasures day after day. Because of this, many yellow dragons maintain friendly relations with local communities of grasslands goblins. The dragons’ propensity to forget or discard yesterday’s treasures complements the kleptomania that strikes many goblins, ensuring the dragon’s hoard never stays the same and forever holds its interest.

entries.name: Maturing
entries: Yellow dragon wyrmlings spend their first few years learning from their parents, who teach the wyrmling how to hunt, where to hunt in lean seasons, and how to avoid grasslands dangers like wildfires and other predators. When a yellow dragon is young, it establishes its own hunting territory on the outskirts of its progenitors’ territories, which includes at least one large rock or outcropping for sunning itself. Adult yellow dragons spend their days alternating between lounging in the warm sun and chasing various creatures in their territory, either to eat or for sport. By the time a yellow dragon becomes ancient, it often has an established relationship with local grasslands peoples, particularly goblins, providing protection in exchange for regular tribute of shiny trinkets or new prey.

entries.name: Young Yellow Dragon
entries: Dragon of Sunlit Hunt and Ambush
A shadow ripples the tall grass as a dragon leaps from concealment, its yellow, catlike body blending into the dry grass. It opens its fanged mouth, which glows like the sun.

entries.name: Ancient Yellow Dragon
entries: Ancient Dragon of Sunlit Hunt and Ambush
A shadow ripples the tall grass as a dragon leaps from concealment, its yellow, catlike body blending into the dry grass. It opens its fanged mouth, which glows like the sun.

entries.name: Pseudodragon
entries: Fickle, Pint-Sized Dragon

items.name: Habitat:
items.entry: Coastal, Desert, Forest, Hill, Mountain, Urban

entries: Pseudodragons dwell in scenic wildernesses, preferably where life is easy and prey is small and slow. There they behave like contented wyrms, creating tiny lairs amid ancient trees and rugged cliffs. They fill these lairs with shiny rocks, colorful shells, and unattended treasures that catch their attention, and they guard these hoards fiercely.
Pseudodragons grow to the size of large house cats, and most have red-brown scales. Some have scales with other hues or patterns—markings distinct from those of their larger draconic cousins.

Many magic-users attempt to befriend pseudodragons, hoping to enlist them as familiars. The creatures' intellect and resistance to magic make them excellent companions, and they're considered status symbols in some spellcasting circles.

Many pseudodragons prefer the finer things in life. These diminutive dragons might be inclined to aid those who ply them with treats. Contrariwise, mages who don't properly pamper their pseudo dragon familiars might be abandoned without warning. Roll on or choose an option from the Pseudo dragon Treats table to inspire a pseudodragon's taste in gifts.
If you want to keep a pseudodragon happy, get used to thinking of yourself as its familiar.
This cat-sized dragon maintains a cautious crouch, candlelight flickering across its rust-red scales. It raises its stinger-tipped tail defensively before it.

entries.name: Miniature Dragon
entries: These small cousins of true dragons dwell in forests, lairing in hollow trees, stumps, small caves, or other snug, enclosed areas. While they resemble miniature red dragons, their fine scales are a reddish-brown in color, and their tails end in a venomous stinger. Pseudodragons are also much less aggressive and more skittish than their larger cousins. When desperate, they can deliver a nasty bite with their needle-sharp teeth, but they rely on their stingers for hunting prey and defending themselves, as their venom can incapacitate even large opponents.

entries.name: Telepathic
entries: Pseudodragons possess a limited form of telepathy that communicates simple emotions or states to others, such as agreement, anxiety, discomfort, acceptance, hunger, or similar. These communications can also take the form of a series of images.

entries.name: Favored Familiars
entries: Wizards consider pseudodragons quite desirable familiars. Their bonding is more complicated than garnering a standard familiar, because pseudodragons view this as a partnership rather than servitude. They often must be persuaded with offerings or bribes.

entries.caption: Pseudodragon Treats
rows: Flamboyant accessories it can wear.
Mementos from a lost friend or master.
Outlandish delicacies—like axe beak-egg omelets or mammoth-milk cheese.
The possessions of a sibling, rival, or master.
Shiny gifts, from gems to abalone shells.
Soft bedding and stuffed toys.
A specific cook's signature dessert.
Time-consuming beauty treatments.
To hear a bedtime story or favorite song.
Trophies and important-sounding titles.

entries.name: Scorch Drake
entries: Fire-Breathing Reptilian Hunter

items.name: Allies:
items.entry: Typically encountered solo, with others of its kind, or rarely works with other creatures.

entries: The dragon’s body is covered in shimmering scales of red, orange, and yellow. Its eyes glow like hot coals and fire burns in its throat as it roars.

entries.name: Roasted Prey
entries: The scorch drake roams charred wastelands and volcanic plains, hunting anything it can catch. Unable to digest fresh kills, scorch drakes roast victims with their fiery breath before dining. They often scavenge prey baked or cooked by other means, such as geothermal activity, the fire breath of other dragons, or even a desiccated body left for days in the sun. In this way, they conserve energy, reserving their potent fire breath for only the choicest meals.

entries.name: Roaming Hunter
entries: Scorch drakes often leave their stony wasteland homes to find more plentiful prey, moving into neighboring grasslands or forests. However, their hunting style often turns those fertile lands into charred wastes. Because they are unbothered by heat and fire, they often start wildfires to flush out prey, gobbling up scorched remains or ambushing prey fleeing advancing flames.

entries.name: Liquid Fire
entries: Scorch drakes radiate heat and are difficult to see clearly at a distance due to the shimmer the heat creates. They exhale a spray of liquid that ignites upon contact with air, which resembles the fire breath of other dragons. However, this liquid is more akin to alchemist’s fire, and it coats and clings to the drake’s prey, causing victims to continue burning.

entries.name: Drider
entries: Spiderlike Underdark Hunter

Driders combine the features of drow and giant spiders. The wicked god Lolth is fond of transforming her drow worshipers into driders, as either a blessing or a curse. These driders often become fanatical servants of their god, or they are overwhelmed by their transformation and live only to indulge their predatory arachnid instincts.

Driders also appear when whole communities are transformed by a wicked god's wrath or other magical means, or driders might be part of a world's natural population. Most dwell underground or in dense forests where they can make the most of their spiderlike traits. Driders with non-drow features are uncommon but possible. Roll on or choose a result from the Drider Metamorphoses table to inspire how supernatural driders come into being.
The drow smiles, its eyes gleaming silver in the darkness. Six more points of silver appear on its forehead as it lowers itself from the cavern ceiling, revealing its arachnid body.

entries.name: Abyssal Origins
entries: The first driders were drow elves who wronged the demon lord of spiders and were transformed as punishment for their transgressions. They have since become their own people that inhabit the dark caverns of the world. Driders sometimes ally with drow, duergar, and other denizens of the underworld, living alongside the smaller folk in their settlements, but driders most often keep to themselves in tight-knit family groups.

entries.name: Underworld Centaurs
entries: Like their surface-world mirrors, driders roam the underworld and are suited for its environment. They can climb walls and traverse ceilings without effort, and where many centaurs have human features, driders nearly always have elven features.

entries.name: Arachnid Bodies
entries: Driders’ lower bodies more than simply resemble spiders; they also have many arachnid features. Their abdomens contain spinnerets for creating webbing, their legs contain sensory organs for detecting movement on webs, and their foremost legs contain poison, like spider mandibles.

entries.caption: Drider Metamorphoses
colLabels: The Drider Gained Its Form As...
rows: A blessing from a deity of assassins, dangerous wildernesses, or the Underdark.
A curse from a powerful hag, vengeful witch, or strange Artifact.
An experiment by an aboleth, a mind flayer, or another life-shaping magic-user.
A magical means of escaping disaster or some worse fate.
A mutation after exposure to chaotic planar energies or strange Underdark radiations.
A punishment from a spiteful god, like Lolth or the Queen of Air and Darkness.

entries.name: Dryad
entries: Tree-Bound Guardian of Nature

items.name: Allies:
items.entry: Other Fey, awakened plants, forest-dwelling Beasts, sprites, treants, unicorns, wood heralds

entries: Guardians of the woodlands, dryads magically flit from tree to tree and from root to bough, harrying trespassers with tangling vines and thorns. Most of these elusive beings have a special connection with one plant or a natural sanctuary that they protect. Some also share physical similarities with the plants they're most connected to. Dryads might sicken or die if their plant or sanctuary is destroyed, recovering only if it is healed or magically replaced. Roll on or choose an option from the Dryad Sanctuaries table to inspire a dryad's bond.
A green woman with barklike skin and bedecked in vibrant green leaves and vines emerges from an ancient oak tree.

entries.name: Forest Defenders
entries: Dryads are fey that are magically bound to trees in the oldest forests of the world, becoming extensions of those trees and defenders of the forest. The death of a dryad’s tree usually results in the death of the dryad, unless the tree’s death was part of the tree’s natural life cycle. A dryad that has survived the death of its tree has one year to find a new, suitable tree with which to bond, or it perishes.

entries.name: Wildlife Allies
entries: When in danger, dryads call on the forest’s animals to protect them or cover their retreat. Dryads can also magically convince would-be foresters and hunters to leave the forest in peace or to seek wood and prey elsewhere. This often leads to conflicting stories of dryads as beautiful and charming or ferocious and accompanied by armies of savage wildlife.

entries.name: Helping Lost Travelers
entries: Dryads aren’t always inimical to creatures hailing from outside their forests. They often help creatures lost in the woods, especially children or humanoids that show respect to nature. Dryads understand that kindness shown to lost travelers today might lead to broader respect and care for the forest in the future.

entries.caption: Dryad Sanctuaries
colLabels: The Dryad Dwells in and Protects...
rows: An acres-large clonal colony—a stand of identical, interconnected trees.
A fortress-like tree, like a baobab or sequoia.
A living lock—a plant that seals evil below or blocks the path to a dungeon.
A lonely tree that stands atop a windswept mountain or amid a petrified forest.
A plant with magic fruit or remarkable seeds.
A shambling mound or treant that the dryad lives in or around as a Fey symbiote.

entries.name: Duergar
entries: Grim Underworld Dwarf of Harsh Discipline

items.name: Allies:
items.entry: Deep gnomes, derro, drow, kobolds, stone giants

entries: The milky white eyes of the stony-skinned dwarf scan the nearby caverns. It then slams its large pick into the cavern wall, knocking loose a bit of metal ore, which it tosses into a nearby cart.

entries.name: Militaristic Dwarves
entries: As dwarves who believed in martial law over a sense of community, the duergar broke away to form their own settlements, traveling deeper into the underworld and away from other dwarves. Their society is comprised of castes, and movement up or down depends on an individual’s skills and contribution to the military, including military service. Duergar didn’t fully forsake the family units of their overworld cousins, but replaced them with bonds within their military family. Many platoons of troops form lifelong bonds and raise children together.

entries.name: Utilitarian Wares
entries: Duergar craft supports their army, from defensible architecture, to weapons that rarely dull, to gleaming armor. Preferring function over form, duergar goods rarely feature embellishments or designs.

entries.name: Bastion Against Horrors
entries: When they broke away from their overworld cousins, duergar didn’t fully embrace a militaristic society until they encountered the denizens of the underworld: abyssal fiends, ghoulish undead, aberrant monstrosities, and many other reality-bending horrors. Duergar regularly battle these horrors, seeing the near-endless struggle as a test of their hardiness and commitment to their life below ground, and they have learned techniques, both to evade the horrors and increase their size to go toe-to-toe with the beasts.

monsterFluff.name: Air Elemental

entries.name: Air Elemental
entries: Primal Spirit of Wind and Storm

items.name: Habitat:
items.entry: Desert, Mountain, Planar (Elemental Plane of Air)

items.name: Allies:
items.entry: Cloud giants, djinn genies, harpies, giant eagles

entries: Energetic spirits from the Elemental Plane of Air, air elementals gather clouds and winds into ever-changing bodies with indistinct limbs and vague features. Beyond their home plane, these elementals might serve magic-users who conjure them, or they might congregate around nexuses of unbridled planar energy, such as wind-scoured mountain peaks or endless storms. In battle, air elementals batter enemies with powerful gusts or transform into whirlwinds to fling away foes.
Air elementals often have distinctive compositions. Roll on or choose a result from the Air Elemental Compositions table to inspire the elemental's appearance.
What can withstand the storm's scream? The lightning's spear? The want of sweet breath? Air is the mightiest of elements—respect its power.
entries.by: Husam, Son of the Breezes, Ruler of Djinn

entries: A cyclone howls as it picks up and scatters debris before settling in a vaguely humanoid shape.

entries.name: Sentient Storms
entries: Air elementals are gathered collections of air, wind, and planar material from the Plane of Air powered by an energetic intelligence. They are most at home on the Plane of Air, but they often find themselves slipping into the Material Plane when a spellcaster summons them or when great thunderstorms on the Material Plane briefly split the barrier between the two planes.

entries.name: Always Moving
entries: Air elementals are full of energy and rarely stay stationary. An air elemental having a conversation might dart around its conversational partner, punctuating each sentence with a halt and tiny thunderclap. When engaging in combat, air elementals similarly dart around their opponents, pausing long enough to attack with a lash of wind or surround and toss opponents.

entries.caption: Air Elemental Compositions
rows: Cumulus or cirrus clouds.
A mixture of vibrantly colored gases.
A pungent, sour-looking miasma
Shifting cloud clusters that resemble animals and simple shapes.
Sinister features obscured in a misty mass.
Swirling storm clouds.

entries: Elementals are incarnations of the elements that make up the universe: air, earth, fire, and water. Though little more than animated energy on their own planes of existence, they can be called on by spellcasters and powerful beings to take shape and perform tasks.

entries.name: Living Elements
entries: On its home plane, an elemental is a bodiless life force. Its dim consciousness manifests as a physical shape only when focused by the power of magic. A wild spirit of elemental force has no desire except to course through the element of its native plane. Like beasts of the Material Plane, these elemental spirits have no society or culture, and little sense of being.

entries.name: Conjured by Magic
entries: Certain spells and magic items can conjure an elemental, summoning it from the Inner Planes to the Material Plane. Elementals instinctively resent being pulled from their native planes and bound into service. A creature that summons an elemental must assert force of will to control it.

entries.name: Bound and Shaped
entries: Powerful magic can bind an elemental spirit into a material template that defines a specific use and function. Invisible stalkers are air elementals bound to a specific form, in the same way that water elementals can be shaped into water weirds.

entries.name: Elemental Nature
entries: An elemental doesn't require air, food, drink, or sleep.

monsterFluff.name: Earth Elemental

entries.name: Earth Elemental
entries: Primal Spirit of Soil and Stone

items.name: Habitat:
items.entry: Mountain, Planar (Elemental Plane of Earth), Underdark

items.name: Allies:
items.entry: Deep gnomes, hinn genies, stone giants

entries: Primal spirits from the Elemental Plane of Earth merge with rocks and minerals to form earth elementals. These beings possess powerful limbs and coarse features, sometimes studded with ore, gems, crystals, colorful striations, or living plants. On the Material Plane, earth elementals often serve those who conjure them, or they appear in regions influenced by their home plane, such as crystalline nodes, energetic fault lines, or veins of magical ore. Earth elementals effortlessly move through stone and can bring ruin to whole structures with their mighty fists.

Earth elementals are typically made of more than dirt. While an elemental's composition doesn't change its statistics or have monetary value, it makes each elemental distinct. Roll on or choose a result from the Earth Elemental Compositions table to inspire an earth elemental's features.

The foundations of our homes, the strength of our weapons, the vaults of our greatest secrets—earth is nothing less than the grip of reality itself. It is the mightiest element. This cannot be denied.
entries.by: Kabril the Perfect Compass, Ruler of Dao

entries: The ground rumbles and shifts, forming two large fists, then a pair of legs, then a small head with gemstone eyes and a cavernous mouth.

entries.name: Sentient Soil
entries: Earth elementals are collections of soil, rock, gemstones, and planar material from the Plane of Earth powered by a quiet intelligence. They are equally at home on the Plane of Earth and the Material Plane, dwelling in the depths of the soil and rock of both planes.

entries.name: Plodding Pace
entries: Earth elementals move and change as quickly as the earth around them, often lying comfortably for decades in the same location. When circumstances force them to act, however, they act with powerful swings of their rocky fists, and the very earth around them rumbles and moves at their call. Many would-be bandits have discovered too late that some small mountain villages are unknowingly protected by earth elementals when they attempt to harm villagers, who incidentally tended plants on the elemental’s back for decades or who once sang to the elemental years ago.

entries.caption: Earth Elemental Compositions
rows: Colorful mineral formations.
Cooled magma in melted heaps.
Grass, moss, or plant roots.
Heaps of peat or decaying matter.
Mounds of sand studded with shells.
Rubble or pieces of a ruined structure.
Striking striations or bands of color.
Veins of iron or other ore.

entries: Elementals are incarnations of the elements that make up the universe: air, earth, fire, and water. Though little more than animated energy on their own planes of existence, they can be called on by spellcasters and powerful beings to take shape and perform tasks.

Like beasts of the Material Plane, these elemental spirits have no society or culture, and little sense of being.
