entries.name: Medusa
entries: Snake-Haired Recluse with a Petrifying Gaze

With their hair of living snakes and their infamous petrifying gazes, medusas are hubristic creatures that inhabit sites of fallen glory. They often dwell beyond the fringes of civilization or travel in disguise, leaving trails of petrified victims. Some medusas dominate groups of monsters or criminals, controlling them with threats of petrified doom, while others recruit servants that are immune to being petrified, such as gargoyles and gorgons.
Medusas are born or created through preternatural circumstances. Roll on or choose a result from the Medusa Fates table to inspire what led to a medusa's creation.
The woman suddenly pulls back the hood of her cloak to reveal hair formed of twisting serpents. Her eyes flash with a golden light, and the snakes begin to hiss.

entries.name: Snake-Haired Connoisseurs
entries: Medusas are human‑like creatures with snakes sprouting from their heads in place of hair. They are clever, charismatic, and have a fierce love of art, music, and finery. Medusas are most known for their deadly gaze, which can turn flesh into stone. Indeed, a few have posed as talented sculptors, though these restrict their work to animal subjects.

entries.name: Hidden Enclaves
entries: Most medusas dwell in remote areas, where their gaze doesn’t sow chaos. They carve elaborate sculptures, play music on harps or flutes, and weave colorful clothes. They are also drawn to art made by others. Many otherwise reclusive medusas risk exposure by sneaking into cities or villages to observe and enjoy such art.

entries.name: Criminal Masterminds
entries: Some medusas use their natural charm, deadly gaze, and near-agelessness to create and run spy networks, criminal organizations, or information brokerages in major cities. They remain masked and hooded when interacting with clients or agents, and they use names or titles meant to impress, such as Matron of Mysteries, Starlit Shadow, or Master of Ashes.

entries.caption: Medusa Fates
rows: Born a medusa and lives unaware of whatever curse or circumstances afflicted its ancestor.
Created by a god and tasked with guarding a treasure or secret.
A cultist who made a fiendish bargain and enjoyed rewards that have since faded.
An explorer transformed and compelled to defend a cursed ruin.
A vain noble whose magical attempt to gain eternal beauty backfired.
The victim of a bite from a magical serpent or reptilian god in disguise.

entries.name: Mephits
entries: Malicious Elemental Hooligans

Mephits are mean-spirited tricksters that dwell on the Elemental Planes. The six most prominent types of mephits resemble halfling-size gargoyles with wings, exaggerated features, and bodies composed of two elements. Most live self-interested existences, indulging their warped senses of humor or overblown egos on their home planes of existence. Some serve as messengers or spies for genies or magic-users.

Mephits resent leaving the elemental extremes where they make their homes. If loosed on the Material Plane or other realms, they lash out with nasty pranks or by tormenting weaker creatures. When destroyed, mephits explode in a burst of elemental magic.
I am Seamusxanthuszenus, Slayer of Fiends, Merchant Most Excellent, Purveyor of Death!
entries.by: Seamusxanthuszenus, smoke mephit with a typically inflated impression of itself

entries: The clouds of dust and steam cackle as they form into two figures, each with sharply pointed ears, long noses, and clawed fingers and toes.

entries.name: Mischievous Elementals
entries: Capricious and hopelessly intrusive, mephits are small elemental creatures native to the various Elemental Planes. A group of mephits is called a “pest,” and indeed, many creatures that regularly interact with mephits think the term might be much too kind. They tend to be chatty, overeager creatures that leap before looking, and they rarely understand the concepts of manners or belongings. Mephits are much more interested in having fun—if that makes someone else sad, it’s not their problem.

entries.name: Questionable Origins
entries: The inhabitants of the Elemental Planes believe mephits are either a natural phenomenon like elemental roaches or mice or fey spirits trapped in elemental bodies. None of the inhabitants of the Elemental Planes want to claim the mephits as their friends or creations, a fact that doesn’t bother mephits.

entries.name: Summoned Familiars
entries: Mephits are commonly summoned by spellcasters needing a mixture of elements to complete alchemical processes or an assistant with a touch more magic or elemental power than a typical familiar. Some mischievous spellcasters feel a kinship with mephits and take a mephit as a familiar. Such spellcasters

entries.name: Explosive Deaths
entries: Each mephit fuses two different elements, leaving its body somewhat unstable. When a mephit is slain, this unstable mixture explodes, as the elements separate and return to their raw states.

entries.name: Various Elements
entries: Mephits can be comprised of any two elements, but the four most common mephits are dust (air and earth), ice (air and water), magma (earth and fire), and steam (fire and water). All mephits share many personality traits, but the elements comprising a mephit subtly affect its personality, causing it to exhibit mild traits related to its component elements. For example, magma mephits tend to be quicker to anger than other mephits, and dust mephits go with the flow of events around them more often than other mephits.

entries.name: Mephits
entries: Mephits are capricious, imp-like creatures native to the elemental planes. They come in six varieties, each one representing the mixture of two elements.

Ageless tricksters, mephits gather in large numbers on the Elemental Planes and in the Elemental Chaos. They also find their way to the Material Plane, where they prefer to dwell in places where their base elements are abundant. For example, a magma mephit is composed of earth and fire, and it favors volcanic lairs, while an ice mephit, which is composed of air and water, favors frigid locales.

entries.name: Elemental Nature
entries: A mephit doesn't require food, drink, or sleep.

entries.name: Merfolk
entries: Sea-Dwelling Humanoid of Coral Realms

items.name: Allies:
items.entry: Dragon turtles, hippocampi, merrow, selkie, water elementals

entries: The graceful creature combines a human torso with a fish tail, fins and whisker-like tendrils on its arms and face, and fin-ears that flap idly in the current. It tightens its grip on a spear and crinkles its brow in a wary expression.

entries.name: Undersea Folk
entries: Merfolk are sea-dwelling and water‑breathing humanoids who live in scattered ocean kingdoms consisting of majestic, underwater cities. Merfolk kingdoms vary in depths from coastal and coral reef kingdoms to deeper sea kingdoms, but even the deepest merfolk kingdoms don’t go into deep ocean trenches, where light fades away and krakens dwell.

entries.name: Seaside Allies
entries: Merfolk seldom visit the surface, but some seaside towns have generations-long trade agreements with merfolk cities near them. Such towns often find fish plentiful in the nearby waters, and their fisherfolk and merchants are left unmolested by denizens of the deep. These unspoken benefits typically remain only so long as the merfolk’s’ relationship with the seaside town remains pleasant and equitable for both sides—and so long as the townsfolk don’t harm, pollute, or overfish the waters.

entries.name: Seaborne Equipment
entries: Forging metal underwater requires magic, dedicated air-filled chambers, or similar accommodations. Because of this, most merfolk grow coral into armor and weapons and use shells, shark jaws, jellyfish stingers, and similar objects as weapons. Those merfolk who do wear or wield metal tools acquire them by raiding sunken ships or trading with surface-dwellers.

entries.name: Guardians of the Depths
entries: The sea contains millennia of history in the form of sunken ships, drowned cities, and castaway ancient treasures. Merfolk make use of these locations, and aquatic adventurers often employ merfolk guides for exploring them. The sea also contains more dangerous locales: deep prisons for ancient, fell creatures held in magical slumbers as well as gates that seal unstable rifts to the Elemental Plane of Water or Lower Planes. Many merfolk kingdoms dedicate themselves to preventing such creatures from reaching the surface world or keeping such rifts carefully guarded, and some consider this dedication a sacred duty.

entries.name: Planar Variations
entries: The proximity of many merfolk kingdoms to planar rifts produces variations in merfolk eggs, causing one in every several dozen merfolk to hatch as a much larger, stronger, and typically more aggressive merfolk known as a merrow. Most merfolk kingdoms treat merrow as any other member of merfolk society, but some merrow reject merfolk society, are exiled from some kingdoms purely for being merrow, or otherwise become separated from their merfolk cousins. These lone merrow occasionally find themselves working for sea hags, sahuagin monarchs, or even humanoid sea captains.

entries.name: Merrow
entries: Ogreish Undersea Abductor

items.name: Allies:
items.entry: Dragon turtles, hippocampi, merfolk, selkie, water elementals

entries: Vicious aquatic hunters, merrow combine the features of ogres with those of primeval, predatory fish. They lurk in coastal waters, hoping to snare unsuspecting prey by bursting from the water and grabbing their quarry or by skewering victims with deadly harpoons. These hunters then drag land dwellers back to dismal undersea lairs. Merrow often keep prisoners in their larders as future meals.

Merrow raid coastal settlements and merfolk communities to steal weapons and treasure. This leads to conflicts between merfolk and merrow, but it also provokes misunderstandings with surface dwellers who blame merfolk for merrow attacks.
Sages trace merrows' origins to aquatic ogres, depraved merfolk, and worse. Such broad theories reveal little about these monsters but overmuch of the dread lurking beyond our certain shores.

The graceful creature combines a human torso with a fish tail, fins and whisker-like tendrils on its arms and face, and fin-ears that flap idly in the current. It tightens its grip on a spear and crinkles its brow in a wary expression.

entries.name: Planar Variations
entries: The proximity of many merfolk kingdoms to planar rifts produces variations in merfolk eggs, causing one in every several dozen merfolk to hatch as a much larger, stronger, and typically more aggressive merfolk known as a merrow. Most merfolk kingdoms treat merrow as any other member of merfolk society, but some merrow reject merfolk society, are exiled from some kingdoms purely for being merrow, or otherwise become separated from their merfolk cousins. These lone merrow occasionally find themselves working for sea hags, sahuagin monarchs, or even humanoid sea captains.

entries.name: Mimic
entries: Shape-Shifter Disguised as an Unassuming Object

items.name: Allies:
items.entry: Typically encountered solo, with others of its kind, or rarely works with other creatures.

entries: In their natural forms, mimics are little more than roaming stomachs, their blobby bodies covered with alien eyes and teeth. They can alter their color, texture, and dimensions to duplicate inanimate objects of their approximate size. Mimics use their disguises as both camouflage and bait. Once victims draw close, mimics strike, lashing out with their sticky pseudopods and toothy mouths. After consuming victims, mimics usually relocate, change form, and await their next meal.
Use the following list to inspire mimics' shapes:

items: Altar
Bell
Boulder
Cauldron
Chair
Chandelier
Chest
Cot
Door
Floor mat
Giant gemstone
Gravestone
Heap of leaves
Keg
Ladder
Lectern
Mannequin
Mirror
Obelisk
Oversize cake
Panel of levers
Pile of bones
Potted plant
Row of books
Sarcophagus
Sculpture
Ship's wheel
Sign
Stalagmite
Stump
Table
Tapestry
Taxidermy
Throne
Topiary
Weapon rack

entries: A hateful crimson eye opens on the surface of the otherwise innocuous barrel. Then another eye opens, and another still. With startling speed, the barrel becomes a pseudopod-wielding horror, its toothy maw opening wide.

entries.name: Shapeshifting Ambusher
entries: The dungeon delver’s bane, a mimic is a stealthy predator that transforms itself into an inanimate object, perfectly copying the appearance of wood or stone. It typically poses as furniture, doors, stairs, and the like. In its natural form, it resembles an amorphous blob with flesh-like protrusions resembling objects it previously copied.

entries.name: Deceptive Hunters
entries: While not terribly intelligent, mimics are uncanny predators. They lurk in well trafficked areas of dungeons, wildernesses, or urban environments, assuming shapes intended to lure prey. They then use their sticky adhesive skin to trap their next meal.

entries.name: Taste Focused
entries: Mimics are supremely patient, but they are obsessed with consuming prey to relish the taste, not necessarily to sate hunger. The taste buds of a mimic’s tongue are extremely sensitive, and they gather a great deal of information with every bite.

entries.name: Minotaur
entries: Bull-Headed Warrior of Maze and Clan

items.name: Allies:
items.entry: Axe beaks, rhinoceroses, grassland or urban-dwelling Humanoids

entries: Charging with axe in hand, this broad and fierce-looking creature has the head and hooved legs of a bull and the body of a towering human.

entries.name: Bullish People
entries: Minotaurs fuse features of both humans and bulls. They can be as stubborn and temperamental as their bull heads imply, but they are also typically very protective of their families and homes. They can be steadfast allies or relentless enemies.

entries.name: Maze Adepts
entries: Mazes hold an honored place in minotaur culture, and many of their most important ceremonies involve mazes in some fashion. A minotaur always remembers its route in a maze and has a knack for choosing the correct direction.

entries.name: Architects and Axes
entries: The minotaurs’ comfort in mazes often extends into a passion for architecture, carpentry, stonemasonry, or similar interest in building objects and structures. As a people, minotaurs excel in building complex structures both above and below ground, and many find pleasure in a well-built structure with a myriad of rooms, hallways, and switchbacks, especially structures that might seem mazelike to visitors. In battle, minotaurs tend to wield axes and can destroy siege engines, watch towers, and other structures with a few, well-placed blows.

entries.name: Moon Knight
entries: Moonlit Fey Champion and Wanderer

items.name: Allies:
items.entry: Typically encountered solo, with others of its kind, or rarely works with other creatures.

entries: A being of pure moonlight clad in tarnished armor creates a sword of pale light from its hand. It then raises its shield, to better display the symbol of a crescent moon glinting on the shield’s surface.

entries.name: Moonlit Directives
entries: Moon knights are fey warriors empowered with moonlight. Mysterious even among other fey, moon knights follow inexplicable directives they say are handed down from the moon itself. Many archfey believe their orders come from a rogue archfey who hides in moonlight or even on the moon itself and work against the knights whenever possible. Other archfey happily embrace the knights, using each knight as a champion in the fey courts or as a warrior to fulfill missions elsewhere. Moon knights are loyal to whomever they choose to follow, but they always heed any orders they receive from the moon above all else, even if it threatens their current masters or missions. Though the moon doesn’t call on them often, moon knights know it leads them to some greater destiny.

entries.name: Transformed Fey
entries: Moon knights can spontaneously appear during the full moon in a forest glade or fallow field filled with fey magic, but most moon knights are lesser fey who are willingly transformed after hearing a song or voice in a beam of moonlight. This leads to moon knights with various personalities, as many remember most of their former lives, but all moon knights understand they now serve a higher calling beyond the frivolities of their pasts.

entries.name: Mordovermis
entries: Many-Mouthed Aberrant Tunnel Hunter

items.name: Allies:
items.entry: Typically encountered solo, with others of its kind, or rarely works with other creatures.

entries: A long, leathery worm glides through the air, four fangs spread wide in a mouth that contains a great, golden eye. The skin on its belly folds back like a robe, revealing a writhing mass of slimy tendrils, each ending in a toothy maw.

entries.name: Tube Feeder
entries: A mordovermis has a brown, wrinkled hide and a long body that resembles a worm, ending in a “mouth” surrounded by four fangs. Its single, golden eye rests in the middle of these fangs. Its powerful gaze can leave prey transfixed as it slithers closer, and its tail ends in a barbed stinger to kill transfixed prey. On its underside are dozens of retractable, segmented, hollow tubes, each tipped with gnawing, envenomed teeth. Their toxin liquefies flesh, which the mordovermis slurps up through the tubes after wrapping around its prey.

entries.name: Squeezing Lairs
entries: Mordovermis commonly inhabit old ruins or underground caves. They prefer to lair in areas accessible only by squeezing through small openings, making their resting areas easy to defend. Because of this, they consume their meals away from their lairs and return to their lairs to rest and avoid the light of day. Their most common prey are deer, wolves, humanoids, and other larger creatures they can easily wrap around.

entries.name: Planar Exiles
entries: Mordovermis fled some great conflict on their home world long ago, and have wandered other worlds since then. On most worlds, they have flourished, delighting in the easy prey and the myriad of hiding spaces in the mortal realm. Few living mordovermis still remember their ancient home and fewer still care to return to it, but they are commonly found in the passageways of the Labyrinth.

entries.name: Mummies
entries: Deathless Ancients with Ageless Ambitions

Mysterious rites and mighty faith can tie spirits to their corpses, binding them to their remains for all time. Should their resting places be violated, these beings, known as mummies, reanimate their deteriorating bodies to restore the sanctity of their tombs and punish those who disturbed their rest.

Mummies pursue those who offend them, typically mortals who desecrate their resting places, steal their burial treasures, or defile sites tied to their faith. With undying rage, these ancient corpses go to extreme lengths to avenge themselves and restore what they need to find peace.

A mummy might look frail, but its body possesses supernatural strength, and its gaze can strike fear in the bravest hearts. Those who escape a mummy's grasp might find themselves subject to a terrible curse. Victims of a mummy's curse gradually wither, their bodies rotting away until they're reduced to dust. This curse can be healed only by the Remove Curse spell or similar magic.
The air carries the stench of decay and choking dust as the figure shuffles forward, its withered features barely visible beneath the yellowed funerary cloth wrapped around it.

entries.name: Tomb Guardians
entries: Intelligent undead reanimated by magic or divine will, mummies are created in dry climates through sacred burial rites. Some are granted unlife immediately, while others sleep for millennia until their reanimation is triggered by a specific event, such as the desecration of their tomb.

entries.name: Curse Bearers
entries: Due to the unique nature of a mummy’s creation, it carries a magical curse that rots the flesh of those it touches, eventually turning the creature into dust. In this way, mummies ensure those who intrude into their tombs never escape and can’t be reanimated. Wise tomb robbers steer clear of ancient tombs and rooms filled with mysterious dust.

entries.caption: Mummy Resurrections
rows: Defend a holy site it was created to protect.
Obey the summons of a mummy lord.
Oppose an enemy who has returned to life.
Protect its descendants from an ancient threat.
Punish the progeny of those who cursed it.
Reclaim treasures robbed from its crypt.
Serve whoever speaks the prayer on its tomb.
Slay anyone who sets eyes on it.

entries.name: Mummies
entries: Deathless Ancients with Ageless Ambitions

Mysterious rites and mighty faith can tie spirits to their corpses, binding them to their remains for all time. Should their resting places be violated, these beings, known as mummies, reanimate their deteriorating bodies to restore the sanctity of their tombs and punish those who disturbed their rest.

Mummies pursue those who offend them, typically mortals who desecrate their resting places, steal their burial treasures, or defile sites tied to their faith. With undying rage, these ancient corpses go to extreme lengths to avenge themselves and restore what they need to find peace.

A mummy might look frail, but its body possesses supernatural strength, and its gaze can strike fear in the bravest hearts. Those who escape a mummy's grasp might find themselves subject to a terrible curse. Victims of a mummy's curse gradually wither, their bodies rotting away until they're reduced to dust. This curse can be healed only by the Remove Curse spell or similar magic.
The air carries the stench of decay and choking dust as the figure shuffles forward, its withered features barely visible beneath the yellowed funerary cloth wrapped around it.

entries.name: Curse Bearers
entries: Due to the unique nature of a mummy’s creation, it carries a magical curse that rots the flesh of those it touches, eventually turning the creature into dust. In this way, mummies ensure those who intrude into their tombs never escape and can’t be reanimated. Wise tomb robbers steer clear of ancient tombs and rooms filled with mysterious dust.

entries.name: Mummy Lords
entries: Mummified high priests or divinely appointed rulers can rise as mummy lords. Commanding both the undead and the desert sands, mummy lords rule alongside or in place of the current living ruler. Terrifying though they are, everything they do is, in their minds, for the betterment of their people, their nations, or their religious orders. Among mortals, mummy lords conceal their undead forms with gold, jewels, burials masks, and elegant robes. Mummy lords aren’t all-powerful. The magic animating a mummy lord imbues its heart, typically stored in a canopic jar near where it was first entombed. If this heart is destroyed, the mummy lord is permanently slain, and many mummy lords go to great lengths to ensure the safety of their hearts.

entries.name: A Mummy Lord’s Lair
entries: Mummy lords typically claim lairs in vast tombs or in palaces at the heart of the cities they rule. They surround themselves with opulence, loyal followers, and an abundance of sand, which they use to defend themselves.

entries.caption: Mummy Lord Plots
rows: Open a portal to the past, when its power was at its height.
Perform a ritual that can be attempted only once every eight hundred years.
Reclaim and resurrect a loved one's corpse.
Reconquer the lands that once composed its empire.
Recover the pieces of its lost heart.
Replace its descendant as the ruler of a realm.
Sacrifice a thousand souls to its god in return for true life.
Transform the people of an entire nation into Undead servants.

entries.name: Mycolid Commoner
entries: Communal Fungus Person of the Deep

This small fungus has the white fibrous stalk and soft, spongy head of a mushroom. Beneath its wide, bell-shaped cap, small arms clutch ramshackle gardening tools and crude weapons, while flat leg-stalks move the plant-creature toward any potential threats.

entries.name: Magical Creation
entries: Mycolids sprout in areas infused with unnatural magical energy, such as the fetid bog of a black dragon, the cavernous ruins of the underworld, or a shady glade blessed by an archfey or druid. Regardless of region or climate, commoner mycolids spend most of their lives cultivating a huge variety of mushrooms into a single unbroken chain of interconnected growth.

entries.name: Community First
entries: Mycolids thrive in harsh environments without thought for shelter, culture, art, or history, and instead pour every moment of their relatively short lives into expanding the colony. Few creatures attack the peaceful fungal folk, but those that do find their woody bodies, soft, springy heads, and immunity to pain difficult to overcome. When a colony is threatened, every commoner mycolid fights to the death to protect the collective.

entries.name: Highly Toxic
entries: Though commoner mycolids are safe to handle, creatures foolish enough to eat one may succumb to their potent toxins. In extreme situations, mycolids may let themselves be eaten to kill massive or dangerous creatures and add fertilizer to the colony. Mycolid spores are a powerful defense mechanism as well, and older mycolids have more powerful spores. Even newborn commoner mycolid spores can addle the mind of most beasts.

entries.name: Spore Lord
entries: When a safe area of rich, damp fertilizer can be secured, a commoner mycolid may undergo a rapid change into a more powerful spore-generating form to create a near-endless supply of identical offspring. This form, called a spore lord, doesn’t rule over other mycolids. But while commoners tend to the health of mushrooms and mycolid sprouts, spore lords protect the colony from threats and supply it with a stream of clones. A single spore lord might create dozens or hundreds of clones of itself in its lifetime, and a dozen spore lords from different rings may share a single fertile location without conflict.

entries.name: Magic Channelers
entries: Upon transformation, a spore lord is made larger, stronger, and smarter by the primal magic that spawned it. Spore lords instinctively know how to manipulate the environment through cultivation and magic.

entries.name: Communal Knowledge
entries: All members of a spore lord’s ring share its memories, but commoners don’t quite understand how and why the spore lord is different. Only when separated from the colony can a commoner reach such an understanding, usually moments before it changes into a spore lord itself.

entries.name: Mycolid Spore Lord
entries: Spore-Wielding Fungus Sovereign

This small fungus has the white fibrous stalk and soft, spongy head of a mushroom. Beneath its wide, bell-shaped cap, small arms clutch ramshackle gardening tools and crude weapons, while flat leg-stalks move the plant-creature toward any potential threats.

entries.name: Magical Creation
entries: Mycolids sprout in areas infused with unnatural magical energy, such as the fetid bog of a black dragon, the cavernous ruins of the underworld, or a shady glade blessed by an archfey or druid. Regardless of region or climate, commoner mycolids spend most of their lives cultivating a huge variety of mushrooms into a single unbroken chain of interconnected growth.

entries.name: Community First
entries: Mycolids thrive in harsh environments without thought for shelter, culture, art, or history, and instead pour every moment of their relatively short lives into expanding the colony. Few creatures attack the peaceful fungal folk, but those that do find their woody bodies, soft, springy heads, and immunity to pain difficult to overcome. When a colony is threatened, every commoner mycolid fights to the death to protect the collective.

entries.name: Highly Toxic
entries: Though commoner mycolids are safe to handle, creatures foolish enough to eat one may succumb to their potent toxins. In extreme situations, mycolids may let themselves be eaten to kill massive or dangerous creatures and add fertilizer to the colony. Mycolid spores are a powerful defense mechanism as well, and older mycolids have more powerful spores. Even newborn commoner mycolid spores can addle the mind of most beasts.

entries.name: Spore Lord
entries: When a safe area of rich, damp fertilizer can be secured, a commoner mycolid may undergo a rapid change into a more powerful spore-generating form to create a near-endless supply of identical offspring. This form, called a spore lord, doesn’t rule over other mycolids. But while commoners tend to the health of mushrooms and mycolid sprouts, spore lords protect the colony from threats and supply it with a stream of clones. A single spore lord might create dozens or hundreds of clones of itself in its lifetime, and a dozen spore lords from different rings may share a single fertile location without conflict.

entries.name: Magic Channelers
entries: Upon transformation, a spore lord is made larger, stronger, and smarter by the primal magic that spawned it. Spore lords instinctively know how to manipulate the environment through cultivation and magic.

entries.name: Communal Knowledge
entries: All members of a spore lord’s ring share its memories, but commoners don’t quite understand how and why the spore lord is different. Only when separated from the colony can a commoner reach such an understanding, usually moments before it changes into a spore lord itself.

entries.name: Guardian Naga
entries: Enduring Serpentine Lore Keeper

Guardian nagas are immortal, serpentine scholars that possess perfect memories. They collect the histories and lore of those they live among, guarding cultures' stories and passing them on to new generations with infallible accuracy. Guardian nagas that outlive their host civilizations might linger in whatever ruins remain, preserving the civilizations' stories so their lost people might live on.
Roll on or choose a result from the Guardian Naga Lore table to inspire what a naga knows.
The regal creature slithers forward on a snake-like tail that extends from its torso, the tail’s blue and gold scales glistening in the light. It smiles and gestures toward the temple.

entries.name: Regal Serpents
entries: Nagas are serpentine creatures with humanoid torsos. Their gold and blue scales and skin give them an otherworldly air of nobility and serenity. They possess great intellect and magical ability, and they have a strong connection to the Upper Planes.

entries.name: Temple Guardians
entries: Directed long ago by some long-forgotten deity, nagas protect ancient temples in deserts or jungles. Occasionally, nagas protect underground temples, either carved below ground or sunken in some ancient catastrophe. Many seeking wisdom or enlightenment make pilgrimages to these temples to speak with the nagas there. Because of this, nagas are rarely alone and most have a retinue of apprentices, monks, priests, and mystics.

entries.name: Driven and Vain
entries: Nagas are driven and highly motivated creatures, whose nigh-unmatched zeal is tempered only by the decades or centuries that some tasks take to complete. They are also vain creatures that bask in the adoration of others. Their need for adoration further drives the naga to provide wisdom and to encourage others to spread the glory of its deity, temple, or sacred site.

entries.name: Immortal Beings
entries: As semi-divine creatures, nagas are immortal. They don’t feel the touch of the years and can’t perish from old age. Similarly, while they can’t be permanently killed except by powerful magic, they also can’t have offspring. Despite this, new nagas occasionally appear to replace nagas permanently slain. Most often, these young appear as large golden eggs found in naga temples—if protected and warmed, these divine gifts hatch as young nagas within a month.

entries.caption: Guardian Naga Lore
rows: The last words of an ancient sage or leader.
The location of a hidden city or continent.
A magic word, password, or riddle's answer.
The names of all who have told it stories.
An otherwise forgotten ritual or spell.
Recipes using regional ingredients.
Stories of forgotten gods and local spirits.
The vulnerabilities of a legendary monster.

entries.name: Spirit Naga
entries: Spiteful Serpentine Grudge Keeper

Spirit nagas loathe the world and all creatures. Possessing perfect memories, these venomous, cobra-like creatures recall every slight committed against them during their immortal existences. In their dank, joyless lairs, they create vicious plots to avenge themselves against even petty offenses.

Spirit nagas seek to claim what they believe they deserve. Their schemes often involve poisons, vile spells, cursed objects, or magical compulsions, eventually making them wellsprings of diabolical knowledge and evil inspiration. Other villains often seek out spirit nagas as advisers and allies. Roll on or choose a result from the Spirit Naga Grievances table to inspire what motivates a spirit naga's schemes.
The regal creature slithers forward on a snake-like tail that extends from its torso, the tail’s blue and gold scales glistening in the light. It smiles and gestures toward the temple.

entries.name: Regal Serpents
entries: Nagas are serpentine creatures with humanoid torsos. Their gold and blue scales and skin give them an otherworldly air of nobility and serenity. They possess great intellect and magical ability, and they have a strong connection to the Upper Planes.

entries.name: Temple Guardians
entries: Directed long ago by some long-forgotten deity, nagas protect ancient temples in deserts or jungles. Occasionally, nagas protect underground temples, either carved below ground or sunken in some ancient catastrophe. Many seeking wisdom or enlightenment make pilgrimages to these temples to speak with the nagas there. Because of this, nagas are rarely alone and most have a retinue of apprentices, monks, priests, and mystics.

entries.name: Driven and Vain
entries: Nagas are driven and highly motivated creatures, whose nigh-unmatched zeal is tempered only by the decades or centuries that some tasks take to complete. They are also vain creatures that bask in the adoration of others. Their need for adoration further drives the naga to provide wisdom and to encourage others to spread the glory of its deity, temple, or sacred site.

entries.name: Immortal Beings
entries: As semi-divine creatures, nagas are immortal. They don’t feel the touch of the years and can’t perish from old age. Similarly, while they can’t be permanently killed except by powerful magic, they also can’t have offspring. Despite this, new nagas occasionally appear to replace nagas permanently slain. Most often, these young appear as large golden eggs found in naga temples—if protected and warmed, these divine gifts hatch as young nagas within a month.

entries.name: Spirit Nagas
entries: Most nagas are temple guardians and known as guardian nagas, but other types of nagas exist. The most common naga outside of the guardian naga is the spirit naga, a corrupted and fallen guardian naga. A spirit naga is a guardian naga that failed to protect its temple from destruction, became corrupted when its temple was desecrated, or succumbed to its vanity and sought godhood. These spirit nagas spend their first decades after their fall seeking vengeance, even going so far as to blame pilgrims for giving them “too much” adoration. Afterwards, they find new purpose in causing misery to those around them, sharing the emptiness and utter loss they feel with others. To this end, they use their considerable power to pretend that they are still guardian nagas, projecting the image of their former selves over their withered, skeletal forms. They take the greatest pleasure from the disbelief that comes when they reveal their true selves to creatures they have been manipulating for years.

entries.caption: Spirit Naga Grievances
rows: A character is to blame for its recent failures.
It has been evicted from its rightful home.
Locals have reneged on an age-old bargain.
Other creatures are mocking it.
A rival is spying on it.
Someone's treasure rightfully belongs to it.

entries.name: Nightmare
entries: Dread Steed of the Lower Planes

Nightmares resemble horses with flaming manes, burning hooves, and smoldering eyes. They terrorize weaker creatures and often ally with denizens of the Lower Planes in committing evil acts. These supernatural horses can innately travel between the Ethereal Plane and the Material Plane, and many know the locations of portals to the Lower Planes, the Shadowfell, and other sinister realms.

Nightmares' speed, resilience, and ability to gallop between planes of existence make them steeds coveted by evildoers. Roll on or choose a result from the Nightmare Riders table to inspire what might employ a nightmare steed.

No mortal horse neighs with such a hellish, echoing sound–and this one’s fiery mane and tail flicker and flap in the wind. It paws the air with flaming, skull-shaped hooves, and fire burns in its eyes.

entries.name: Fiendish Horses
entries: Nightmares are intelligent, equine fiends found on the Lower Planes, where they roam in vast herds. They can run in the air as easily as they do upon the ground, and their hoofprints burn briefly wherever they step.

entries.name: Powerful Mounts
entries: Nightmares are the favored mounts and battle companions of devilish generals, demon lords, powerful undead, and even high priests of fire, planar, demonic, or devilish deities. Such creatures need only approach a nightmare with the request for assistance in dark deeds, and the nightmare often happily obliges. Mortals must perform complex rituals to call nightmares to their service, and in these situations, nightmares almost always require the supplicant prove it is worthy of a steed as magnificent and powerful as the nightmare. The desired proof is unique to each nightmare, but it typically involves the performance of a dark deed or a display of magical strength.

entries.name: Planar Striders
entries: Nightmares were first created as messengers of dark deities and powerful fiends, who granted them the power to move quickly between the Lower, Material, and Ethereal Planes. This helped the nightmares enter almost any fortress, especially the citadels of fiends, and so a portion of the power was eventually stripped from them. Still, nightmares can move between planes and can bring those nearby with them, making them highly desired as mounts and messengers.

entries.caption: Nightmare Riders
rows: The champion or messenger of an evil deity.
A group of joyriding imps or quasits.
An innocent soul trapped on the wild Fiend.
A lore-hunting mage, cultist, or lich.
A night hag herding larvae between planes.
A wicked cavalier, such as a death knight, an erinyes, an incubus, or a vampire.

entries.name: Nimbostratus Spirit
entries: Storm-Chasing Spirit of Cloud and Rain

items.name: Allies:
items.entry: Bronze dragons, sea hags, storm giants

entries: A cloudy wisp, covered in snow and crackling with electricity, zips about the clouds with a laugh that sounds like rain lightly striking a metal surface.

entries.name: Cloudy Fey
entries: Nimbostratus spirits are fey creatures who live in the sky, dancing between raindrops, chasing lightning, and sleeping on clouds. They delight in stormy weather and sometimes descend to the ground to observe the full force of a storm.

entries.name: Storm Chasers
entries: In their quest to frolic among the clouds, nimbostratus spirits tirelessly follow storms until they dissipate. The normally carefree fey become despondent if they find themselves in clear skies. To enjoy plenty of lightning, thunder, wind, and precipitation, they often follow cloud and storm giants or ask friendly druids to call up rain.

entries.name: Lightning Javelins
entries: When forced to, nimbostratus spirits prefer to fight their battles at a distance. This allows them to throw electrically charged javelins while their hazy appearance shields them. When they become bored with fighting or desperate to flee, they use their magic and the cover of clouds to escape.

entries.name: Heralds of Storms
entries: Wise druids, sea captains, and weather mages study nimbostratus spirits, cataloguing individual spirits and their storm preferences, to help them predict inclement weather in mountains and at sea. Some nimbostratus spirits are known to be especially wild and reckless, and their appearance can mean major storms, lightning, and floods. Some believe that a fey Prince of the Clouds summons these spirits once or twice a year to generate and empower especially violent storms.

entries.name: Ochre Jelly
entries: Multiplying Amoeboid Hunter

items.name: Allies:
items.entry: Typically encountered solo, with others of its kind, or rarely works with other creatures.

entries: Ochre jellies are giant, yellow-brown amoebas that digest organic creatures. They tirelessly hunt any prey smaller than themselves, oozing over, under, and around obstacles in their path. Once they overwhelm their quarry, these acidic slimes dissolve the flesh, hair, and scales of their prey, leaving behind clothing, equipment, treated leather, and bone.

If damaged by lightning or a slashing weapon, an ochre jelly splits in two. These smaller jellies work together to consume foes, but afterward they move on to hunt independently. Both eventually grow into full-size jellies.
What ochre jellies can't dissolve they leave behind. Roll on or choose a result from the Ochre Jelly Leftovers table to inspire such remains.
The viscous, yellow substance suddenly rises, extending tendrils outward. The stench of rotten eggs fills the air as it moves.

entries.name: Stormy Origins
entries: Occasionally, rocs lay eggs that never hatch, because of damage to the egg or improper hatching conditions. When such an egg is struck by lightning from a storm magically conjured by a storm giant’s shifting mood, the yolk within transforms into an ochre jelly. The jelly eventually breaks free from the shell and escapes the nest, driven by the roc’s instinct to hunt and consume meat.

entries.name: Rotten Bodies
entries: Ochre jellies are living, rotten eggs and exude the overwhelming stench of weeks-old eggs. The stench precedes them wherever they go and leaves those close to them retching uncontrollably. They use this to their advantage, pouncing on and devouring victims of their awful stench. Their acidic bodies dissolve most creatures they come across, but the acid they produce doesn’t harm harder materials, such as bone, wood, or metal.

entries.name: Lightning Affinity
entries: Created by magical lightning, ochre jellies are inherently immune to it. In addition, when an ochre jelly is struck by lightning or is cut in pieces, it splits in half, creating two, autonomous jellies. Each jelly then continues hunting, consuming, and growing until it regains the size of the original jelly. In this way, ochre jellies have propagated across the world, even in regions that don’t contain rocs or storm giants.

entries.caption: Ochre Jelly Leftovers
colLabels: After a Meal, the Ochre Jelly Leaves Behind...
rows: A bone etched with a word or an eerie symbol.
Broken dragonborn or tiefling horns.
An ornate prosthetic limb.
The skeleton of an explorer's pet (perhaps a small dog, monkey, or parrot).
A skull with gold teeth worth 1d4 GP.
A spotless suit of metal armor.

entries.name: Ogres
entries: Raging Hulks and Hoarders

items.name: Habitat:
items.entry: Arctic, Desert, Forest, Grassland, Hill, Mountain, Swamp, Underdark

items.name: Allies:
items.entry: Ettins, bugbears, giant boar, goblins, worgs

entries: Ogres are selfish raiders and hulking gluttons spawned of hateful supernatural forces. From dismal ruins and bleak hinterlands, they raid vulnerable communities and ambush travelers. Ogres covet food and treasure, and they spitefully destroy art, books, clockwork devices, and other delicate or lovingly made things. Occasionally they kidnap victims to eat later or, more rarely, performers who catch their interest.

Ogres trace their origins to wrathful deities such as Erythnul, Takhisis, and Vaprak. They magically emerge from the earth of lands corrupted by evil gods, sinister magic, or ancient curses. Some bear evidence of the places that spawned them, sporting rocky calluses, mossy growths, or frozen scars.
The enormous creature stomps forward, its thick hide marked with battle scars. It gives a toothy grin through a coarse, dark beard, then swings a massive club around its head.

entries.name: Little Giants, Big Humanoids
entries: Ogres are the smallest of the giants and often look like bulky, thick-skinned humans. Omnivorous and hardy, they can survive almost anywhere, and many dwell in the wilderness in small, nomadic groups. Just as many ogres live solitary lives, seeking their own paths.

entries.name: Great Strength
entries: Ogres are best-known for their sheer strength, as even the weakest of ogres is stronger than the average human. They enjoy contests of strength and often forget how strong they truly are. Many ogres use that strength to earn a living in cities, finding steady work as stevedores, haulers, bouncers, or bodyguards. Some ogres work in cities only during droughts or when herds decline, while other ogres find that life in the city suits them.

entries.name: Shock Troops
entries: Because of their strength, ogres are highly valued as shock troops for military excursions. Many nations actively seek ogres for their armies, and soldiering often works out well for an ogre. After leaving an army’s regular food and shelter, many ogres turn to banditry to survive.

entries.name: Oni
entries: Wickedness Drawn to the Wicked

Oni are elusive entities that inhabit dark forests and other wildernesses. By shape-shifting into the form of an innocent or moving invisibly, oni encroach on communities and lonely roads. They frequently harass people of faith, testing the limits of their piousness, or torment selfish people, punishing them for their wickedness. Wise communities often have guardian statues, annual rituals, or local superstitions meant to keep oni at bay. In rare cases, an oni might gradually befriend such communities and protect them from other threats for generations.
Oni torment villages that don't pay them or other supernatural forces respect. Roll on or choose a result from the Oni Troubles table to inspire how an oni menaces such communities.
Floating in midair, this large, blue creature wears ceremonial armor and carries a massive glaive in one clawed hand. Its solid white eyes glint with intelligence as its hand glows with magic.

entries.name: Ogre Mages
entries: Oni are nearly immortal giants known for their cunning and magical powers. Long ago, oni were magically adept ogres who made a deal with an archdevil. The archdevil imbued the ogres with power but also physically altered their forms to be closer to its image, shifting their skin to a pale blue, sprouting horns from their heads, and turning their hair and eyes white. Not long after, the archdevil perished, and oni now possess fiendish power without the constraints of an infernal contract.

entries.name: Hidden Schemers
entries: Oni can magically change their shapes and often hide among humanoids and ogres. They have little patience for decades-long schemes and like seeing the immediate fruits of their plotting. These plots can vary wildly between oni, from some oni bettering their own personal lives to others inciting war between nations.

entries.name: Otherworldly
entries: Though oni feel most at home on the Material Plane, their fiend-granted power marks them as creatures of the Lower Planes. When an oni is killed, its spirit is transferred to the Lower Planes, where it revives as if it were a devil or demon. Because of this, oni are comfortable taking greater risks, knowing they can easily return to continue their schemes regardless of whose ire they earn.

entries.caption: Oni Troubles
rows: Charming people to perform nasty tricks.
Claiming a bridge, gate, shrine, or trail and trying to eat anyone who comes near.
Luring other monsters to the settlement.
Playing drums that keep everyone awake.

entries.name: Orc
entries: Fierce Raider of Clan and Warband

items.name: Habitat:
items.entry: Forest, Grassland, Hills, Mountain, Swamp, Underground

items.name: Allies:
items.entry: Other orcs, hawks, mastiffs, war horses

entries: The tusked humanoid roars a challenge with its axe and shield raised, ready for comba.

entries.name: Ancient Warriors
entries: Orcs sing battle songs of former empires, slain foes, and military brilliance. Though no sages can confirm these stories, they serve as the foundation for the orcs’ austere way of life and unshakable honor code.

entries.name: Hardy Wanderers
entries: Most orcs are driven by a thirst for exploration and discovery. Due to their uncanny survivability, far-traveling orcs have been found near-dead in dangerous locales, only to make a seemingly miraculous recovery days after being found.

entries.name: Orc Warlord
entries: Conquering Leader of Orcish Warbands

items.name: Habitat:
items.entry: Forest, Grassland, Hills, Mountain, Swamp, Underground

items.name: Allies:
items.entry: Other orcs, hawks, mastiffs, war horses

entries: The tusked humanoid roars a challenge with its axe and shield raised, ready for comba.

entries.name: Ancient Warriors
entries: Orcs sing battle songs of former empires, slain foes, and military brilliance. Though no sages can confirm these stories, they serve as the foundation for the orcs’ austere way of life and unshakable honor code.

entries.name: Hardy Wanderers
entries: Most orcs are driven by a thirst for exploration and discovery. Due to their uncanny survivability, far-traveling orcs have been found near-dead in dangerous locales, only to make a seemingly miraculous recovery days after being found.

entries.name: Otyugh
entries: Garbage-Heap Gourmand

Otyughs live to eat—the more disgusting the meal, the better. They consider all non-otyughs that come within reach dishes in life's endless buffet. In dumps, sewers, polluted ruins, and similar murky depths, otyughs devour garbage, carcasses, and anything else their tentacles can cram in their expansive maws. Some creatures ply otyughs with trash to recruit them as watchful—if disgusting—guardians.

Otyughs often bury themselves amid trash heaps and observe their surroundings with their eye-studded stalk. They use glittery trash and telepathic urgings to coax creatures close, then burst from hiding, attacking with their spiny tentacles and filthy maws. Roll on or choose a result from the Otyugh Lures table to inspire how an otyugh tempts prey close.
An enormous mound rises from the muck, grasping with two tentacles as a massive, toothy maw opens on its front.

entries.name: Tentacled Mass
entries: This bizarre creature’s body is a shapeless mass of flesh atop three thick fins. Three tentacles sprout from the body, two longer ones ending in spiky, grasping pads, and a third, shorter limb bearing the otyugh’s eyes and nostrils. Much of the creature’s front side is taken up by a cavernous mouth filled with sharp teeth.

entries.name: Adaptable Appetites
entries: Otyughs are omnivorous, eating anything organic and delighting in rot, waste, and carrion. Their constant hunger extends to living creatures as well. They hide while submerged in water or bury themselves in mud or filth, lashing out at potential prey as they pass.

entries.name: Damp Dwellers
entries: Otyughs prefer wet environs, lairing in swamps, rainforests, partially flooded caves, or other wilderness areas prone to damp and decay. They can also be found in populated areas. City sewers, village middens, or boneyards contain what these sewer horrors view as abundant sources of food.

entries.caption: Otyugh Lures
colLabels: To Attract Potential Meals, the Otyugh...
rows: Disguises its tentacles with garbage puppets.
Sings an enticing song in Otyugh.
Telepathically transmits a message like "Happy good stuff here!" or "Help now! I'm too delicious?"
Telepathically transmits an image of a large gemstone, crooked weapon, or soggy pastry.

entries.name: Owlbears
entries: Magically Perfected Predators

items.name: Allies:
items.entry: Typically encountered solo, with others of its kind, or rarely works with other creatures.

entries: Created long ago by misguided mages, owlbears combine keen avian eyes, thick feathers, and a tearing beak with a mighty bearlike frame. Despite their magical origins, owlbears have propagated and spread to wildernesses across the multiverse.
Owlbears dwell in distinctive dens. Roll on or choose a result from the Owlbear Den Features table to inspire an owlbear den's noteworthy traits.
Bursting from the forest undergrowth, a massive creature—equal parts bear and owl—gives a roaring screech as it charges.

entries.name: Ursine Avians
entries: Vicious carnivores, owlbears devour vast amounts of deer, boar, and other creatures in the forests they inhabit—and vomit up distinctive bezoars of undigested bone, hide, and feathers. An owlbear has the shape and mass of a grizzly bear, with a thick coat of fur intermixed with feathers. The massive body is topped with a great, owl-like head, complete with large, round eyes and a curved, hooked beak that can grip and tear its prey. Though incapable of flight, the owlbear’s limbs are covered with enough feathers for gliding when it leaps from sufficiently high cliffs and hills.

entries.name: Territorial
entries: Territorial and testy, owlbears are quick to challenge interlopers in their domain. An owlbear faces larger or more numerous opponents with undaunted savagery that often scares off even more powerful foes.

entries.name: Nesting
entries: Normally solitary, owlbears remain together only to rear hatchlings until they are old enough to fend for themselves. Mated pairs sometimes build an enormous stick-nest in a cavern or on a rocky spire. An angry owlbear defending its eggs or young is especially dangerous.

entries.name: Tribal Guardians
entries: When humanoid tribes capture owlbear hatchlings or eggs, they sometimes raise the creatures as fearsome guardians or mounts. Dwarves have also been known to keep owlbears as guards.

entries.caption: Owlbear Den Features
rows: Evidence of previous occupants, like bandits, wolves, or dragons.
Heaps of regurgitated pellets studded with coins or other treasure.
A nest with 1d6 owlbear eggs.
Passages through the earth or hollow trees.

entries.name: Pegasus
entries: Elusive Winged Steed

items.name: Allies:
items.entry: Angels, blink dogs, and righteous Humanoids

entries: Winged, sapient horses of noble bearing, pegasi are as majestic as they are elusive. Most avoid the affairs of other creatures, preferring to dwell amid idyllic pastures or floating islands, or on other planes of existence. Others serve deities of the Feywild and Upper Planes, aiding heroes in need. In rare cases, pegasi might befriend virtuous people and serve as their companions and steeds.

Pegasi are hunted by servants of evil, leading many of these winged steeds to flee strangers on sight. Roll on or choose a result from the Pegasus Offerings table to inspire how one might show their good intentions to a wary pegasus.
Pegasi are the cherished steeds of our creator, Corellon. To see one is a blessing, but to ride one proves nothing less than the love of the gods.
A noble flying horse, its coat shining like newly fallen snow, soars overhead on majestic, feathered wings, mane and tail streaming behind it.

entries.name: Flying Horses
entries: Free-spirited creatures, pegasi soar the skies with wild dignity on the Upper Planes and in the Material Plane. Ranging far from civilization, pegasi are skittish creatures that are typically comfortable only in the presence of their own kind. Intruders cause them to take wing quickly to find more secluded surroundings. Their diet is similar to mundane horses, feeding on grass and flowers in alpine meadows and taking water from mountain streams and high lakes.

entries.name: Mountain Nests
entries: Fiercely protective of their young, pegasi build nests in rocky hills, atop spires and peaks inaccessible to most creatures without the power of flight. When the young are ready to leave the nest, the entire herd watches over and protects them until they reach adulthood.

entries.name: Heroic Servitors
entries: Pegasi are intelligent, autonomous, and untamable. However, a being of pure heart and good intent can sometimes convince a pegasus to become its companion, especially if the being is in desperate need or faces some monumental task, such as thwarting those committing evil acts.

entries.caption: Pegasus Offerings
rows: Bearing the gear of a hero the pegasus aided.
Offering magical fruit or holy spring water.
Singing a song in Celestial, Druidic, or Sylvan.
Wearing the garb of an ancient heroic order.

entries.name: Phase Spider
entries: Plane-Shifting Arachnid Ambusher

items.name: Habitat:
items.entry: Desert, Forest, Grassland, Hill, Planar (Ethereal Plane), Underdark, Urban

items.name: Allies:
items.entry: Typically encountered solo, with others of its kind, or rarely works with other creatures.

entries: Phase spiders appear out of nowhere to attack, then vanish just as swiftly. These horse-size, magical arachnids are endemic to the Ethereal Plane. From vaporous lairs, they peer through the Border Ethereal into the Material Plane. When they detect prey, phase spiders draw close and then shift or "phase" to the Material Plane to attack. They shift between planes of existence and attack from unexpected directions until they overcome their prey or are forced to retreat.

Phase spiders are more intelligent than mundane spiders, but most are cowards. They usually flee if they're outnumbered by creatures capable of seeing them on the Ethereal Plane or pursuing them there. They make exceptions for ghosts and similar spirits, which phase spiders gain sustenance from and pursue as favored prey.
Some sages say you unknowingly occupy the same ethereally coterminous point as a phase spider an average of four times each year.
A large spider pops into view as if from nowhere, poised on eight legs. Patterns of deep green mark its black carapace. Eight eyes scan for signs of prey before it vanishes again.

entries.name: Ethereal Spiders
entries: Phase spiders are large spiders with an innate connection to the Ethereal Plane, which allows them to slip in and out of the plane with ease. Consummate predators, they can move in and out of sight and reach while constantly tracking their prey. Much smarter than other spiders, phase spiders live in small groups led by a single matriarch and communicate with each other via a language made up of a combination of mandible clicks and body language.

entries.name: Elusive Nests
entries: Phase spiders make their nests in the Ethereal Plane to keep their young safe from predators on the Material Plane. Because of this, phase spider young are particularly vulnerable and reliant upon the adults, and phase spiders are aggressively protective of the young. Few ever see a phase spider nest, and most who do don’t survive the experience.

entries.name: Dog Hunters
entries: Phase spiders despise blink dogs and often kill them on sight. While many sages speculate the phase spider’s shifting causes painful dissonance for blink dogs, some speculate the blink dog’s teleporting is the only natural magic capable of easily reaching phase spider nests. Phase spiders attack the dogs preemptively to protect their vulnerable nests.

entries.name: Purple Worm
entries: What Gnaws the Roots of the World

items.name: Allies:
items.entry: Typically encountered solo, with others of its kind, or rarely works with other creatures.

entries: Titanic purple worms burrow through the earth and sand. Ever ravenous, they devour smaller creatures and ravage entire communities in their aimless burrowing.
Purple worms alone are bad enough, but the blasted monsters have a knack for unearthing things that are even worse!
An enormous worm, its body armored in plates of bluish-purple chitin, bursts from the cavern floor. Its vast, toothy maw is large enough to swallow a horse whole.

entries.name: Underworld Predators
entries: Truly the rulers of their domains, purple worms claim the underworld as their hunting grounds. These tremendous monsters can bore through solid rock, creating new tunnels and passageways as they travel. Any creature a purple worm encounters besides another of its kind is prey.

entries.name: Follow Prey by Sound
entries: Purple worms are attracted by sound and vibration, leading most subterranean humanoids to travel as quietly as possible and ensure that battles are short and decisive. Major settlements in the underworld have contingencies in place for a purple worm attack.

entries.name: Gemstones and Ore
entries: A purple worm digests the earth and rock it ingests while tunneling, excreting it along the tunnels it creates and in the caverns where it rests. In mineral-rich areas, brave souls may find undigested gems and unrefined ores among a purple worm’s waste, or even inside its body if they are mighty enough to defeat it.

entries.name: Rakshasa
entries: Deceiver Hungry for Power and Flesh

items.name: Allies:
items.entry: Urban creatures, especially Humanoids capable of serving it

entries: Masters of manipulation, rakshasas infiltrate communities to claim positions of power. While disguising their true natures, they kidnap victims and indulge their insatiable hunger for flesh.
Rakshasas can withstand some degree of magic, but legends tell of blessed warriors felling them with crossbow bolts, arrows, or similar weapons.

Rakshasas' appearances combine humanlike bodies with the features of animals and monsters. All rakshasas have a physical oddity that remains when they adopt magical disguises, such as palms where the backs of the hands would be on humans.
The clawed fingers of a tiger-headed man dressed in finery tap idly on a book he carries in his back-turned hands. His fanged mouth smiles warmly, but the smile never reaches his cold eyes.

entries.name: Material Fiends
entries: Fiends long separated from the Lower Planes, rakshasas are tiger-headed, lightly furred creatures with backwards-facing hands. They enjoy indulging their appetite for power, physical comfort, and culinary delights.

entries.name: Powerful Manipulators
entries: Rakshasas are masters of deception and coercion. They enact schemes in humanoid societies, manipulating mortals, directing nations, and leading shadow organizations. Their unique magic protects them from all but the most powerful spells, but it makes them strangely susceptible to piercing weapons.

entries.name: Immortal Scheming
entries: Rakshasas are patient and methodical, spending months or even years on schemes. If slain, a rakshasa’s spirit lingers on the Material Plane, reincarnating after its dark whispers corrupt a mortal.

entries.name: Remorhazes
entries: Super-Heated Arctic Arthropods

items.name: Allies:
items.entry: Typically encountered solo, with others of its kind, or rarely works with other creatures.

entries: Remorhazes are centipede-like terrors that burrow through snow and ice to ambush smaller creatures that trespass in their frozen territories.
A bluish-white, segmented insectoid creature rises from the snow, shard-like mandibles snapping. The snow around it vaporizes into steam as the gaps in its carapace glow red with heat.

entries.name: Arctic Terror
entries: The remorhaz is a terror of cold, northern climes. It blends worm and centipede features with a long, segmented body covered in an icy carapace and rows of small legs the color and shape of icicles. Its segmented carapace vents an extreme, internal heat, melting nearby snow and burning prey and predator alike. A remorhaz unable to vent this heat may try to cool its body by lying in ice or snow, but if no cooling is available eventually its own heat cooks its internal organs.

entries.name: Indiscriminate Diet
entries: Remorhazes eat almost anything including elk, reindeer, walruses, and polar bears as well as humanoids and even giants. They occasionally feast on the stone and metal ore required to maintain their protective carapaces. A remorhaz can subsist on a diet of only stone and metal ore for a few weeks, but a remorhaz maintaining such a diet risks its carapace becoming too heavy to move or thickened enough to block its vents, preventing it from expelling excess heat.

entries.name: Debated Origins
entries: Some scholars believe the remorhaz is an odd, arctic off-shoot of the underworld’s purple worm. Remorhazes themselves care little about taxonomy and have devoured many would-be scholars seeking to study them.

entries.name: Robot Drone
entries: Ancient Mechanical Scout

items.name: Allies:
items.entry: Typically encountered solo, with others of its kind, or rarely works with other creatures.

entries: This metallic construct resembles a beetle with three legs spaced equidistantly around an oval body. A jet of blue flame shoots from its underside, keeping the creature aloft as it zips around. The scent of burning oil fills the air around it.

entries.name: Ancient Relics
entries: Robot drones were created by an advanced civilization to spy on enemy forces, their miniature size and ability to fly enabling them to keep tabs on foes without being detected. Robot wardens were constructed as mostly mindless, obedient warriors to protect the populace. Some such civilizations still exist in lost cities, but most robots have outlived their makers.

entries.name: Ruins Guardians
entries: Most robots are now scattered throughout wastelands in ancient bunkers, fallen citadels, and even crashed, otherworldly ships, protecting what remains of the people they once served. Many lie dormant until intruders trespass in the ruins or a curious tinkerer

entries.name: Robot Warden
entries: Ancient Mechanical Ruin Guardian

items.name: Allies:
items.entry: Typically encountered solo, with others of its kind, or rarely works with other creatures.

entries: This metallic construct resembles a beetle with three legs spaced equidistantly around an oval body. A jet of blue flame shoots from its underside, keeping the creature aloft as it zips around. The scent of burning oil fills the air around it.

entries.name: Ancient Relics
entries: Robot drones were created by an advanced civilization to spy on enemy forces, their miniature size and ability to fly enabling them to keep tabs on foes without being detected. Robot wardens were constructed as mostly mindless, obedient warriors to protect the populace. Some such civilizations still exist in lost cities, but most robots have outlived their makers.

entries.name: Ruins Guardians
entries: Most robots are now scattered throughout wastelands in ancient bunkers, fallen citadels, and even crashed, otherworldly ships, protecting what remains of the people they once served. Many lie dormant until intruders trespass in the ruins or a curious tinkerer

entries.name: Roc
entries: Avian of Unbelievable Size

items.name: Allies:
items.entry: Typically encountered solo, with others of its kind, or rarely works with other creatures.

entries: Birds of prey of fantastic scale, rocs hunt over vast territories and can snatch whole elephants, whales, or wagons in their talons. They then carry their prey back to their remote nests, journeys that can take days and cover hundreds of miles.
Rocs nest amid remote heights. Their nests are typically littered with treasure and uneaten prey. Roll on or choose an option from the Roc Nest Remnants table to inspire what's in a roc's nest.
A colossal raptor descends from the sky, its expansive wings blocking out the sun and its thunderous shriek echoing off the mountainsides.

entries.name: Giant Raptor
entries: This massive bird of prey resembles a crested eagle in general form. The color of a roc’s plumage varies, but a typical roc is a golden brown, with white on the chest, tail, and crest. With wingspans of up to 200 feet and bodies as big as dragons, these mighty aerial hunters can snatch up creatures the size of an elephant in their sharp talons. With larger or more dangerous prey, a roc might fly up into the air then release it, feasting on the creature’s remains in the aftermath of the fall.

entries.name: High Eyries
entries: Rocs lair on high mountains or coastal cliffs, building massive nests, often of whole trees ripped from the ground. Rocs are solitary creatures, coming together only briefly to mate. Likewise, once the eggs hatch and the young are fully fledged, they fly far away to build their own nests and establish their own hunting grounds.

entries.name: Fishers
entries: Distant relatives of eagles, rocs frequently hunt marine life like their cousins. A roc might dive at a surfacing whale, snatch the creature out of the water, and carry it back to its seaside nest for a feast. Experienced sailors sailing through known roc territory often chum the waters behind their ships to encourage the rocs to dive for sharks and other marine life instead of deckhands.

entries.caption: Roc Nest Remnants
rows: The burial litter of a lost hero.
A caravan wagon full of trade goods.
A live elephant.
1d4 eggs larger than adult humans.
Someone marooned in the nest.
A statue of a knight riding a rearing steed.

entries.name: Roper
entries: Tentacled Subterranean Trapper

items.name: Allies:
items.entry: Typically encountered solo, with others of its kind, or rarely works with other creatures.

entries: Camouflaged as rock formations, ropers are aberrant ambushers that lurk in wait for smaller creatures. These bizarre subterranean hunters extend their rubbery tentacles to explore and prod their surroundings, often reaching beyond their fields of vision. Should they encounter prey, these limbs ensnare victims and drag them close to ropers' toothy maws. If these tentacles are severed, ropers rapidly grow replacements.

Ropers can move, albeit slowly. Crawling on the sticky cilia that cover their undersides, ropers can climb walls and suspend themselves from ceilings. These hunters often position themselves in unexpected or treacherous locations, using their surroundings to weaken their prey. Roll on or choose a result from the Roper Hazards table to inspire what dangers ropers employ when ambushing prey.
Rule 9: Never trust a stalagmite.
entries.from: X the Mystic's Rules of Dungeon Survival

entries: A spiral of red eyes slowly opens around the top of one of the cavern’s stalagmites. Fleshy tendrils wiggle out of the stalagmite’s sides and whip forward, as a rock-toothed maw opens beneath the eyes.

entries.name: Hungry Cave Formations
entries: Consummate predators of the deepest caverns, the roper’s conical shape and stony hide resemble a stalactite, stalagmite, or similar cave formation. They have limited control over their coloration and can subtly adjust it to better blend in with the surrounding cavern.

entries.name: Patient Hunters
entries: Ropers can scale walls and even ceilings for the perfect spot to ambush unsuspecting prey. Patient and needing only a few meals a week to survive, ropers can sit for hours or even days in one location, waiting for prey.

entries.name: Ropy Tendrils
entries: A roper has a near-endless supply of rope‑like tendrils it can extend from its body. When ambushing prey, it uses these tendrils, which contain a weakening poison, to grab, reel in, and devour prey. The roper can also use these tendrils to help it move along cavern walls.

entries.caption: Roper Hazards
rows: Areas that trigger traps.
Caverns filled with smoke or gas.
dead magic zone or Wild Magic Zone.
The lair of a creature it is trying to bait out.
A nest of rats, insects, or other vermin.
Patches of brown mold* or green slime*.
Pools of magma or boiling water.
razorvine or similar dangerous plants.

entries.name: Rust Monster
entries: Corrosive, Equipment-Eating Scavenger

items.name: Allies:
items.entry: Typically encountered solo, with others of its kind, or rarely works with other creatures.

entries: Rust monsters roam the Underdark searching for ferrous metal. When they find this material—whether natural veins, subterranean structures, or creatures' equipment—these beetle-like scavengers rush to feed. Using their feathery antennae, rust monsters dissolve metals such as iron and steel into rusted scrap. They easily gnaw through this corroded metal using their mandibles. Rust monsters usually ignore creatures without metal equipment, but they defend themselves if attacked.
A strange, insectile creature skitters forward on four legs. It waves two large, feathery antennae about in a questing manner.

entries.name: Rust Eater
entries: As its name implies, this odd creature can rust ferrous metals, and it eats the resulting flakes of metal. The rust monster roams caves and tunnels seeking out such metals. When it finds metal, the creature aggressively rushes to devour the meal. Clever adventurers can often save their weapons or armor by distracting the creature with another source of metal.

entries.name: Armored Shell
entries: A rust monster is about the size of a large dog. It has armored plates, feathery antennae, and four segmented legs. Its thin, whip-like tail thrashes when it is agitated or catches the scent of metals.

entries.name: Color Matches Diet
entries: The color of a rust monster’s hide depends on its diet. A rust monster whose diet includes large amounts of mithral would have a silvery sheen, for example, and a rust monster which eats a great deal of copper might have a green or brown tinge.

entries.name: Sahuagin
entries: Predatory Humanoid of the Hungry Deep

items.name: Allies:
items.entry: Aboleths, chuul, dragon turtles, sea hags

entries: The scaly humanoid advances, a trident held adeptly in its clawed, webbed hands. Thrashing its heavy tail, the creature opens the mouth of its piscine head to reveal rows of razor-sharp teeth.

entries.name: Coastal Terrors
entries: Sahuagins are piscine humanoids approximately the same size as humans, but their bodies are covered in gray and green scales with lighter scales along their chests, abdomens, and throats. A large fin extends from the base of each sahuagin’s skull and partway down its spine. They reside in deep ocean waters and in the waters along coastlines. They are almost exclusively carnivorous, consuming whatever creatures they can catch and kill, which sometimes puts them on the wrong side of coastal humanoid communities.

entries.name: Magical Monarchs
entries: Magical ability is rare among sahuagins, and most sahuagins have a deep respect or sometimes awe of magic. This leads many sahuagins to follow magical creatures, such as sea hags, and to elevate the rare magical sahuagins to leadership positions in their communities. These sahuagin monarchs vary widely in their ideals and goals, and they each lead their communities in their own ways. Some sahuagin monarchs prefer their communities barter and trade with coastal humanoids, relying on those humanoids’ forged metals for weapons and tools, but many sahuagin monarchs send their people to prey on coastal humanoids, encouraging those in their community to take by force whatever metal weapons, tools, and shiny baubles they find. This tendency has earned sahuagins the title of “sea devils” in many coastal humanoid settlements.

entries.name: Sahuagin Captain
entries: Battle-Tested Leader of the Hungry Deep

items.name: Allies:
items.entry: Aboleths, chuul, dragon turtles, sea hags

entries: The scaly humanoid advances, a trident held adeptly in its clawed, webbed hands. Thrashing its heavy tail, the creature opens the mouth of its piscine head to reveal rows of razor-sharp teeth.

entries.name: Coastal Terrors
entries: Sahuagins are piscine humanoids approximately the same size as humans, but their bodies are covered in gray and green scales with lighter scales along their chests, abdomens, and throats. A large fin extends from the base of each sahuagin’s skull and partway down its spine. They reside in deep ocean waters and in the waters along coastlines. They are almost exclusively carnivorous, consuming whatever creatures they can catch and kill, which sometimes puts them on the wrong side of coastal humanoid communities.

entries.name: Magical Monarchs
entries: Magical ability is rare among sahuagins, and most sahuagins have a deep respect or sometimes awe of magic. This leads many sahuagins to follow magical creatures, such as sea hags, and to elevate the rare magical sahuagins to leadership positions in their communities. These sahuagin monarchs vary widely in their ideals and goals, and they each lead their communities in their own ways. Some sahuagin monarchs prefer their communities barter and trade with coastal humanoids, relying on those humanoids’ forged metals for weapons and tools, but many sahuagin monarchs send their people to prey on coastal humanoids, encouraging those in their community to take by force whatever metal weapons, tools, and shiny baubles they find. This tendency has earned sahuagins the title of “sea devils” in many coastal humanoid settlements.

entries.name: Shark Affinity
entries: Sahuagins have an innate connection with sharks. The beasts rarely attack sahuagins, often react protectively toward sahuagins in their territory, and instinctively follow telepathic commands from sahuagins. Shark-riding sahuagin captains lead most sahuagin groups that travel away from their settlements. These captains decorate their clothing with the teeth of the various sharks they’ve commanded and wear necklaces sporting the teeth of their favored shark companions from over the years.

entries.name: Sahuagin Monarch
entries: Royal Tyrant of the Hungry Deep

items.name: Allies:
items.entry: Aboleths, chuul, dragon turtles, sea hags

entries: The scaly humanoid advances, a trident held adeptly in its clawed, webbed hands. Thrashing its heavy tail, the creature opens the mouth of its piscine head to reveal rows of razor-sharp teeth.

entries.name: Coastal Terrors
entries: Sahuagins are piscine humanoids approximately the same size as humans, but their bodies are covered in gray and green scales with lighter scales along their chests, abdomens, and throats. A large fin extends from the base of each sahuagin’s skull and partway down its spine. They reside in deep ocean waters and in the waters along coastlines. They are almost exclusively carnivorous, consuming whatever creatures they can catch and kill, which sometimes puts them on the wrong side of coastal humanoid communities.

entries.name: Magical Monarchs
entries: Magical ability is rare among sahuagins, and most sahuagins have a deep respect or sometimes awe of magic. This leads many sahuagins to follow magical creatures, such as sea hags, and to elevate the rare magical sahuagins to leadership positions in their communities. These sahuagin monarchs vary widely in their ideals and goals, and they each lead their communities in their own ways. Some sahuagin monarchs prefer their communities barter and trade with coastal humanoids, relying on those humanoids’ forged metals for weapons and tools, but many sahuagin monarchs send their people to prey on coastal humanoids, encouraging those in their community to take by force whatever metal weapons, tools, and shiny baubles they find. This tendency has earned sahuagins the title of “sea devils” in many coastal humanoid settlements.
